Three stones bearing colophons, JSY 4, JSY 25, and JSY 32 also were moved and preserved, though the first two were damaged in the process. The boulder behind the pavilion bearing five colophons, however, was completely destroyed, together with a number of other colophons, among them the stone carrying JSY 43.

figure.head: Water carrying blackish residue running over gu 故 (26/1), a 阿 (26/2), and na 那 (26/3); photograph taken in 2006
携帶黑色沉積物的水流淌過“故”（26/1）、“阿”（26/2）、“那”（26/3）字。2006年拍攝

div.p: Fig. shows the trench separated from the sutra on the right by the stone dam topped by several flights of steps. On the left is the stone staircase, now broken into three pieces, which once led up to the escarpment from behind the pavilion.

During the reconfiguration of the topography of Sutra Stone Valley in the mid-1960s a reservoir was left behind the dam on the northern side of the sutra field (figs. and ). On our map of the colophons in the Valley, its original location can still be seen (see part 2, front endpaper). In 1982 this reservoir was filled in (see also fig. ). Possibly because water from it leaked beneath the dam, the weir built of stones over which water had flowed from the reservoir into the trench was replaced by a bridge in two parts, under which the water now flows. The bridge is seen in the background of fig. .
Also in 1982, the stone steps in the central area were fortified by steel hooks to prevent their slippage (fig. ).

Since around 1982, a massive earthen rampart, walled in stone and planted with trees, has bordered the sutra field at its southern edge. From there visitors can look down on the columns and read the characters nearby.
Red paint highlights the engraved strokes, making them more visible from a distance.

The enclosure of the sutra field prevents it from being walked across and thus promotes its conservation, but it is still possible to circumambulate the sutra on all four sides (fig. ). Lost, unfortunately, in the reconfiguration of Sutra Stone Valley is the sound of burbling water that enticed travelers for centuries. Water is still creeping beneath the porous northern dam, leaving a blackish residue as it crosses the sutra field (fig. ). The loss of many colophons is lamentable, and equally to be regretted is the unattractive architecture of the dams, robbing the Valley of its former charm with its calligraphy in various styles, ancient and modern, once felicitously embedded in a pristine natural setting.

After China joined the UNESCO in 1985, Mount Tai was included in the first batch of Chinese monuments that were given the status of World Cultural Heritage 世界文化遺産 in the eleventh session of the World Heritage Committee in 1987.The importance of this decision for China is reflected in an inscription of 1992 on the top of the mountain by Li Peng 李鹏 (1928–1998), at that time Premier of the People’s Republic of China. Here he called for the protection of the world heritage. See 4: 1103. In the volume edited by UNESCO, entitled Cultural Landscapes of Universal Value: Components of a Global Strategy, an essay on Mount Tai (and Mount Hua in Shaanxi) reserves almost an entire page for a photograph of the engraved characters of the Diamond Sutra and states that despite its deterioration, “it is still the most important cultural relic of Mt. Tai.” 124.

figure.head: The trench west of the sutra; photograph taken in 2006
刻經址以西的水渠。2006年拍攝

div.p: In 2001 the State Councile 國務院 listed the Mount Tai Stone Carvings 泰山石刻, which included the Diamond Sutra and the imperial inscription of Tang Emperor Xuanzong on the summit as Major Historical and Cultural Sites Protected at the National Level.As no. 454 in the Fifth Batch of Major Historical and Cultural Sites Protected at the National Level 第五批全國重點文物保護單位. For the entire list, see the State Council Document (Guofa 國發) 2001, no. 25: “國務院關於公布第五批全國重點文物保護單位和與現有全國重點文物保護單位合并項目的通知.” (http://www.gov.cn/gongbao/content/2001/content_60955.htm, accessed October 13, 2020). On this occasion, a video surveillance system was installed to monitor the inscriptions and an inscribed stone was set up explaining the significance of Sutra Stone Valley (JSY 64). Since then the Valley has been under the supervision of the State Council 國務院, the executive branch of the central government in Beijing, thus achieving the highest possible recognition in the realm of cultural heritage in China.

This recognition of the site also engendered new efforts to preserve the inscription. In 2001 Jiang Fengrong 姜豐榮 discovered a fragment of a stone sheet with three characters from columns 27 and 28 of 128 cm length that is now stored at the Palace of the Mother of the Big Dipper (fig. ). It is indicated in fig. with a red outline at the upper edge of section 4B. In early 2002 material was infused into the stone, visible on figs. (below), (above), and (above and below).See . Unfortunately, one year later An Tingshan observed that the surface tension in the areas surrounding the carved characters had become too great, resulting in further cracks damaging the surface and causing the topmost layer of the stone to flake off. 387. Recently, geologists have analyzed the physical and chemical properties of the stone surface in an attempt to find ways to prevent further decay. , .

div.head: 字數統計
div.p: 四百多年以來，經石峪刻經現存字數幾經統計，而結果出入頗大。明萬曆十四年（1586）登岱的王衡（1561－1609）僅記尚存泰半。王衡《重九後二日登泰山記》第386頁。見本卷“文獻輯録”部分，“王衡”條。至18世紀晚期，聶鈫（1711－1796）依據石經字體做出斷代，并從金石學角度加以詳審，尤其是記録了具體字數。而唐仲冕（1753－1827）記録的字數不足二百字，做此估算的具體時間不詳，應在1782年至1793年之間。《岱覽校點集注》上篇：卷11，第363－364頁。見本卷“文獻輯録”部分，“唐仲冕”條。清嘉慶二年（1797），阮元（1764－1849）獲得石經拓片，一紙一字，凡296字。《山左金石志》第14483－14484頁。見本卷“文獻輯録”部分，“阮元”條。20年後，魏源（1794－1857）勘察實地，統計字數爲“大約五百”。《魏源集》第732－773頁。見本卷“文獻輯録”部分，“魏源”條。李佐賢（1807－1876）在清同治十年（1871）清理經石表面的碎石之後，發現901個刻字。見本卷“文獻輯録”部分，“李佐賢”條。這一統計數字被李瑞清（1867－1920）采納，於1916年製出最早的經石峪刻經原狀録文，并於1918年複製印行（）。《泰山經石峪金剛經墨拓》折頁。李瑞清坦稱，刻經原狀録文根據徐乃昌（字積餘，1868－1943）過録吴式芬（子芯，1796－1856）所作録文。張壽平第5頁，將吴式芬名字録作吴式宓。1910年，孫葆田（1840－1911）依據一件尺幅甚大的拓片釋讀了經題以及前30行，連同其他行的31個單字，共計995字。
現存刻字的計數差异取决於金石學家如何計數。1921年，蔣叔南（1885－1934）統計“能拓者”凡960字。蔣叔南第187頁。但一年之後，田士懿（1870－1929?）便完成了當時最完整的釋讀，計1075字。《山左漢魏六朝貞石目》第143－152頁。

figure.head: Li Dongchen’s transcription of the sutra characters including their state of preservation; after 7b
李東辰所抄録刻經及其保存狀况。采自李東辰葉7b

div.p: 金石學家調查經石峪刻字的周詳與否也因人而异。姚光裕在1929年的題跋（經石峪51號）中記其清除了石面的碎石之後，新出73字。1932年，趙新儒（1876－1952）雇人移除覆蓋刻經下方的石塊，記: “共存完整可讀者，一千餘字。”《新刻泰山小史》第52頁。趙新儒還提及在經石峪附近的殘石上找到兩個字。
辨識石面是否有刻字取决於經驗。傅振倫在1931年發表的一篇游記中寫到，有的碑估可以提供943字的拓本，而有的可找到1300個字之多。傅振倫第516－517頁。可找到的字的數量也因季節而异，尤其是夏季，充沛的流水有礙於刻經址的調查工作。
1946年，李東辰首次根據刻字的保存狀况而分類摹録。他提供了46行（含3個空白行）刻字的確切字數，并且精細繪圖，以記録保存狀况（）。李東辰。
1965年，泰山管理處在石堤建成前調查統計1124個字，僅241個字完好無損。張少華圖24。政府於2001年所立經石峪爲國家級保護單位的保護碑中指出，刻經原有2799個字，尚存1313個字（經石峪64號）。經本項目組調查，刻經原有2747個字左右，現有1469個字可識别。見上文，。
如所示，石塊平攤於刻經址北側石面。石塊分布因時而异，或曾掩蓋部分刻經，清同治十年（1871年）李佐賢清理刻經址碎石即爲一例。其後的清理刻經石面工作有案可查，且刻字可識讀者驟增，箇中緣故，此必爲一端。

ab.abbr: 蔡穗玲 王平國 中山耶

div.head: Counting the Characters
div.p:
For more than four centuries the characters of the sutra visible at Sutra Stone Valley have been counted many times, with strikingly different results. Wang Heng 王衡 (1561–1609), who went to the mountain in 1586 stated simply that only half of the inscription remained. 386. See Sources, Wang Heng, p. . In the late eighteenth century, after Nie Qin 聶鈫 (1711–1796) dated the inscription stylistically and scrutinized it epigraphically, specific numbers were reported. Sometime between 1782 and 1793 Tang Zhongmian recorded fewer than 200 characters. 1: juan 11: 363–364. See Sources, Tang Zhongmian, p. . In 1797, rubbings of 296 single characters were given to Ruan Yuan 阮元 (1764–1849) for his research. 14483–14484. See Sources, Ruan Yuan, p. . Two decades later Wei Yuan counted “about 500” at the site itself. 732–773. See Sources, Wei Yuan, p. . Li Zuoxian, having cleared debris from the rocky surface in 1871, found 901 characters.See Sources, Li Zuoxian, pp. . This number of character appears in the earliest reproduction of the layout of the text, dated 1916 and published in 1918 by Li Ruiqing 李瑞清 (1867–1920; fig. ). foldout. Li claimed his transcription of the layout of the sutra was based that of Xu Naichang 徐乃昌 (Jiyu 積餘; 1868–1943), which in turn derived from a transcription by Wu Shifen 吴式芬 (Zixin 子芯; 1796–1856). 5, transcribes the name as 吴式宓.
In 1910, based on a gigantic single-sheet rubbing, Sun Baotian 孫葆田 (1840–1911) transcribed the title and the first 29 columns and added another 31 single characters from the area beyond column 30 (our count, including the title), yielding a total of 995 characters.

Some of the discrepancies in tabulating the extant characters can be attributed to what individual epigraphers considered worth counting. In 1921 Jiang Shunan 蔣叔南 (1885–1934) counted 960 characters “that could be rubbed 能拓者.” 187. But one year later, Tian Shiyi 田士懿 (1870–1929?) made the most complete transcription up to that time, counting 1075 characters. 143–152.

The thoroughness with which epigraphers investigated the inscription also varied. In 1929 Yao Guang­yu 姚光裕 wrote in his colophon (JSY 51) that 73 new characters had come to light after he cleared debris from the stone surface.
In 1932 Zhao Xinru 趙新儒 (1876–1952)
hired workers to remove stones covering the lower parts of the text and reported that “more than a thousand complete and readable characters survived. 共存完整可讀者，一千餘字。” 52. Zhao also claimed to have found two characters on stone fragments some distance from the Valley.

figure.head: Li Ruiqing’s layout text with colophon of 1916 below; after , foldout
李瑞清所製經石峪刻經原狀録文及1916年跋。采自《泰山經石峪金剛經墨拓》折頁

div.p: The ability to discern the presence of characters on the stone surface required experience. Fu Zhenlun reported in 1931 that one shop could offer to rub 943 characters, while some claimed they could find up to 1,300. 516–517. The number accessible also varied with the season, especially in the summer, when abundant flowing water hampered work on the sutra field.

In 1946 Li Dongchen was the first to categorize the characters according to their degree of conservation. He provided the exact numbers of characters for 46 columns (he included the three empty columns in this number) and made drawings minutely recording the state of their preservation (fig. ). .

A survey of 1965 conducted prior to the building of the dam by the Tai Shan Management Bureau 泰山管理處 counted 1,124 characters, of which only 241 were fully preserved.The document is reproduced in fig. 24. The government stele of 2001, announcing that Sutra Stone Valley is protected at the national level, cites 1,313 characters of an original 2,799 (JSY 64). Our survey resulted in 1,469 characters identifiable of about 2,747 originally engraved.See above, p. .

Fig. shows stones lying on the rocky surface to the north of the sutra field. At various times such stones, like those cleared by Li Zuoxian in 1871, may have covered parts of the sutra text itself. Subsequent clearings of the sutra field are recorded and must account at least in part for the dramatic increase in the number of visible characters.

div.head: 《金剛經》刻字的捶拓、雙鈎複製與摹刻
div.p: 民國之前的經石峪《金剛經》拓本已難獲見。17世紀初期，畢自嚴（1569－1638）的旅伴曾令其隨從製作九字拓片供其賞玩。《三叟同游記》第446a頁。見本卷“文獻輯録”部分，“畢自嚴”條。如前文所述，至18世紀末，阮元已經可以獲得其中296字的拓片。見注。
19世紀下半葉，學者努力拓取經石峪刻經，成套拓本所收刻字顯著增加。碎石的清理使頗多刻字重見天日，900餘字的成套拓本垂手可得。見本卷“文獻輯録”部分，“李佐賢”條。

學者江標（1860－1899）在其日記中提及光緒十年（1884）春讀阮元筆記之事，江標提及的阮元筆記，即《小滄浪筆談》卷3：葉40b－41a；見本卷“文獻輯録”部分，“阮元”條。其後於泰山見到一位年長碑估，此人有一套多達800餘字的拓本。這一數量，據江標稱，與阮元著録的296字大相徑庭。《江標日記》第1册：第24頁。江標於此年秋天登臨泰山時，訪《金剛經》摩崖，得見950餘字。《江標日記》第1册：第44頁。有關於此，他參考了《山左金石志》第14483－14484頁。見本卷“文獻輯録”部分，“阮元”條。當江標1899年再登泰山時，以“大泉十千文”購得一套收録950餘字的拓片。《江標日記》第1册：第344頁。

江標於泰安購得此套拓片之際，其他地方的學者和官員則難以獲取此資源。高官潘祖蔭（1830－1890）乃古玩圈中翹楚，羅振玉（1866－1940）聲稱，其所藏潘氏信札記載，潘氏托付友人陳介祺（1813－1884）搜求一套完整拓本。羅氏則幾經周折，在1897年獲取一套他認定的完整拓片。《石交録》第475頁。見“文獻輯録”部分，“羅振玉”條。馬衡（1881－1955）在20世紀20年代依舊聲稱：“深山窮谷，難與椎拓，故完全之本至不易致。”馬衡第84頁。

羅振玉亦與日本最初研究經石峪《金剛經》的學者關係密切。1902年，山本竟山（1863－1934）通過其長輩日下部鳴鶴（1838－1922）的介紹，成爲羅振玉的學生。蘇浩第253頁。山本竟山至少七次前往中國。1903年日下部鳴鶴感謝山本竟山持贈“泰山大字”拓片，山本至此時已兩次拜訪羅振玉。有關日下部與山本的書信往來，見蘇浩第86－88頁。日下部或許挑選了這些拓片，用作《書談》一文的插圖。日下部鳴鶴第21－22頁。
民國時代，經石峪《金剛經》拓片愈發易得，雖有政令嚴禁隨意拓製，但未嚴格執行。見上文。 1946年，李東辰具體記録了經石峪拓片的交易：
div.quote: 邑中業碑帖者，大宗交易品爲經石峪拓片。春秋佳日，椎拓者往；夏雨冬霜，相率停工。近年事業雕零，几無問津者。拓片一紙一字，分爲三種，曰全套，曰不重字，曰集聯集句。拓法有墨拓，有朱拓。墨拓用𤇆墨爲之，朱拓用銀硃或品紅和蛋白爲之。因山風最厲在峪，衹拓字口，歸而重行修正，補拓周圍。一日能成百餘字者，曰能手。李東辰葉6a－b。
div.p: 由此可知，拓工製作不同標準的成套拓片，其質量迥异。大多數的單字拓片交由古玩商兜售。傅振倫（1906－1999）於1931年發表的文章記載，拓片在泰安街頭隨處可見。傅振倫第517頁。據一份1936年的旅游指南，經石峪《金剛經》拓片成爲泰安西門大街各家書鋪備受青睞的旅游商品。《泰山旅游便覽》第17－18頁。在相距頗遠的外省，古玩商居然備有經石峪《金剛經》拓片現貨。在一份1921年杭州西泠印社可供書目中，成套拓本的定價爲30銀元，無重複單字的成套拓本價格爲10銀元，而四言或者五言集聯的價格爲4角或6角（一角合1/10銀元）。《西泠印社金石印譜法帖藏書目》第625頁。西泠印社創建於1904年，時任會長爲著名書畫家吴昌碩（1844－1927）。

單字拓片已在18世紀晚期作爲法帖加以收藏。金棨收藏了拓本，并於嘉慶二年（1797）爲泰安的一座道觀殿宇以其拓本作爲法帖題寫了木質匾額。《泰安文物大典》第64頁和周郢第1126－1127頁。19世紀初，單字拓片作爲旅游商品和禮品大受歡迎，徐宗幹（1796－1866）記曲阜縣丞王大淮（1785－1844）持贈經石峪吉字拓片，并認爲是王氏供職蒙族官員松筠（1752－1835）官邸時所得，見《斯未信齋文編》第335頁。松筠生平，見《清代名人傳略》第691－692頁。李慈銘（1830－1894）在其1890年日記中記載道，他從一位訪客處得到了“阿膠四匣”“彘脯”“白棗”以及“經石峪集字聯”。見《越縵堂日記》第17册：第12627頁。據此後記載，這些拓本通常爲四字或八字集聯。例如，建築史家關野貞（1867－1935）藏有一套可能其在1907年山東考察之際所獲的八字拓片，組成具有儒家内涵的四字對聯：“知命樂天，樹德種義。”上聯語出《易經》，是對乃父關野峻節（號“樂天”）的致敬。《十三經注疏》上册：第77頁。有關關野峻節，見常盤大定。不久後他即送下聯予常盤大定（1870－1945）。關於二人的交往，見本卷上册《經石峪刻經研究史》一文，以及。 語言學家與書法家後藤朝太郎（1881－1945）1925年在北京市場獲購一些拓片，還爲自己的朋友、藝術家與商人伊吹震（1888－1961）選購三張拓片，可組成其名號“老大樹”。後藤朝太郎第248頁。有關後藤朝太郎，見傅佛果第200－208頁。

拓片可供鑒賞，在多種場合亦用作裝飾。其中極爲常見的是集聯。在中國與日本，對聯可裝飾住宅與書房，以及政治與宗教組織的會客廳。湖南衡陽市石鼓書院入口曾有一副由經石峪《金剛經》集字而成的儒家格言對聯：“修名千佛上，至味五經中。”（）此聯的張掛時間不詳，毁於1944年戰火之中。早在19世紀中葉，經石峪《金剛經》拓片集字已開始公開展示。陝西巡撫張祥河（1785－1862）於道光二十九年(1849)充任陝甘鄉試監臨，秦中貢院以經石峪《金剛經》集字而成的儒家格言對聯作裝飾。張祥河在其《壬子秋闈即事·千佛摩挲經峪字》句的夾注提及此事。見張祥河第540頁。格言可能刻於木板上，懸挂於壁。此刻拓片於2017年出現於一拍賣會上（https://web.artfoxlive.com/product/673357.html，查詢於2021年8月3日）。對聯題跋作：“集泰山經石峪北齊人摩崖懸秦中至公堂。道光已酉(1849)科鄉監臨陝西巡撫華亭張祥河識。”官員潘衍桐（1841－1874）在其客廳懸挂“南園”二字木製匾額。官員邵日濂（1854－1929）在其詩《訪潘嶧琴同年》中提及，見《續修四庫全書》本《兩浙輶軒續録》第1687册：第42頁。 此舉在19世紀載録仍不多見，至民國時期則儼然廣爲流行。涉川玄耳在1916年的旅行記中提及：此種由單字拓片裱成的集聯常用於裝飾沙龍或書房。涉川玄耳第12頁。見本卷“文獻輯録”部分，“涉川玄耳”條。正如1922年的報告所言，中華歸主協會懸掛有其名稱的四字拓本，并將平版印刷而成的複製品作爲横向與縱向的標志張懸於上海分行的建築之上。《泰山經石峪説略》第189頁。
位於早稻田大學會津博物館的四字集句“名著大作”拓片爲横幅裝裱，最近重新懸掛於曾用作早稻田大學圖書館書庫的大廳入口處，即其最初位置。拓片圖版及説明見《早稻田大學圖書館藏資料圖録》第275頁。此横幅的今昔照片，見https://www.waseda.jp/culture/aizu-museum/news/2017/10/17/1867/（2021 年9月24日訪問）。松平康國（1863－1945）於1904年把在中國所獲拓片捐贈早稻田大學。松平康國被委任爲時任直隸總督袁世凱的譯員後前往中國。他的游記提及這些拓片的由來。見松平康國第34頁；本卷“文獻輯録”部分，“松平康國”條。該校圖書館第一任館長、古物學家市島春城（1860－1944）從中選出了這四個字。 展示了在著名作家及東京帝國大學文學院教授夏目漱石（1867－1916）故居的一次聚會，時爲1916年12月，即夏目漱石去世後的一個月。他的家人、朋友、知識精英和同事相聚於漱石山房，談論他的最後時光及其精神遺産。他的主室擺放一件摺叠屏風，而《金剛經》拓片裱裝其上。關於這張照片上的人物姓名，參見夏目鏡子第7頁。關於這張照片和另一張可見此屏風的照片，見松岡譲圖版101和102。此屏風於東京漱石山房紀念館重建的書房展出。 數位訪客提及了這引人注目的拓片，反映了經石峪《金剛經》書法在日本頂級文化圈中的聲譽和名望。幾位作家曾提到這些拓片。芥川龍之介（1892－1927）經常參加在其偶像夏目漱石家中舉辦的雅集，他提過一架屏風。根據松岡譲（1891－1969）的描述，一共有兩架屏風，一架位於入口處，另一架在書房。見芥川龍之介第386頁和第487頁，以及松岡譲第278－279頁。以上所述恰好展現了采自大幅書法的漢字之功用。對《經石峪金剛經》摩崖在紀念碑式書法話語體系中的作用的討論，參見本卷上册《經石峪刻經研究史》一文，。

在經石峪，游人也可直接聯繫拓工，訂製拓片。1922年，一群日本訪客於經石峪搜求單個吉字，如“壽”“福”等字，以及“福德圓滿”之類習見吉語，并且現場製作。泉對信之助第485－486頁。亦見本卷討論，涉川玄耳，。中國游客同樣好此舉。徐悲鴻（1895－1953）是康有爲的入室弟子，也是名畫家，他於1931年與一位同事帶領畢業參觀團計14名學生前往泰山。徐悲鴻熟知乃師對《金剛經》摩崖的讚賞。其一行人休憩於經石峪，而臨近離開時，徐悲鴻仍不願停止勘視刻字。魏敬群。他挑購了八張拓片，集聯爲“獨特偏見，一意孤行” 。張華。

散見於世界各地的《金剛經》拓片往往不名由來，有一件則不然。佛學研究者常盤大定由山東帶回的拓片大都今收於日本。常盤大定於1921年10月11日到訪經石峪，并在數天之後將拓片運往東京。常盤大定第218－219頁。 然而，這些拓片基本上毁失於1923年12月1日關東大震灾和此後的二戰之中，渡邊健哉第40－41頁；石川禎浩第12頁。僅102張單字拓片幸存。常盤大定將其贈予山形縣三川町宗蓮寺。此寺主持本多藏界（1843－1918）之女千代是其夫人。石川禎浩。本堂方格天花板上張貼 90張拓本，12張拓本則位於客間欄間（）。天花板上的拓本指向不同方向，不成文句。此處的《金剛經》拓片裝置於佛教氛圍。這與中國大多數展示場所相比别有异趣。
拓本提升了經石峪《金剛經》的聲譽，但也傳播着不准確的視覺信息。僅有極少數的拓片由金石學家爲嚴肅的學術研究而製作。拓工則不由分説，采用種種瞞天過海的技倆，提高拓片的製作産量，例如拓製之後再在拓片上加墨，以使刻字外廓更爲清晰。涉川玄耳第42頁。
製作拓本的行爲雖然看起來是純粹的機械複製刻銘，但刻石由三維形狀轉變爲二維媒介時，其視覺信息也隨之消失殆盡。

figure.head: Detail of strokes of characters ru 入 (24/12) and bu 不 (24/13) that were damaged after the carving; photograph taken in 2006
刻後損毁的“入”字（24/12）和“不”字（24/13）的殘迹。2006年拍攝

div.p: 一張攝於1929年的照片記録了三個拓工的工作場景（）。如李東辰所見，拓工首先大體勾勒刻字輪廓，之後上墨於周圍的紙張，以形成捶拓效果。此照片顯示，刻字在垂直的石壁上重新刊刻，拓片由此而得（經石峪58號）。據説一些拓工會在家中的灰泥墻上重新切割刻字，以免來回奔波之苦，但其製作低劣。張彦生第84頁；宗鳴安第393頁；魏龍圖第83頁。有關同一字兩種拓本的比較，見本田春玲第55－57頁。 時至今日，這些刻字以原形複刻於岱廟的一塊巨石（），以及庭院中一堵墻的石板（）。

拓工還會有意使刻字扭曲變形。如孫葆田所言，爲了獲得統一尺寸的刻字，以便釘於建築物的柱子之上，拓工會故意壓縮或拉長筆畫。因單字拓片不能保留刻字的空間關係，而這些拓片并無獨特標識，其原初順序容易打亂。爲避免此問題，孫葆田曾委托拓工用一張大尺幅紙張整拓前29行刻經。見本卷“文獻輯録”部分，“孫葆田”條。孫葆田所記葛山摩崖（葛山1號）。《中國佛教石經·山東省第二卷》第217－218頁，也依靠“整拓原行”。見《山東金石志》第9254－9255頁，英譯參見尤麗第144頁。
凡此皆表明，雖然拓片是研究經石峪《金剛經》必不可少的素材，但必須謹慎使用。拓片記録的刻字字形或許因時而變，亦揭示了拓工通過改變筆畫的形狀或捏造新的刻字以對拓片“整形”。

除了拓片，唐代複製法書的“雙鈎”技法也用於複製《金剛經》刻字。見雷德侯第34－35頁。此種技法見於姜夔（1155－1221）《續書譜》“臨摹”篇。《續書譜》第300－301頁。楊守敬勾勒了《金剛經》刻字的筆畫輪廓，而未填墨。此種勾勒本以《縮摹泰山石經峪字》一名刊刻於清光緒三十三年（1907）（）。書牌作“光緒定爲（1907）刊於鄂城”，但書前有清宣統元年（1909）序。楊守敬還以此種方式複製《石頌》（鐵山2號）。見《中國佛教石經·山東省第二卷》第27頁。當代研究山東石刻的專家歐陽中石與安廷山1986年版《楊守敬集》第10册“前言”中贊楊守敬鈎填頗得刻字神韵。楊守敬在序言中聲稱，“惟字體過大，不便臨習，乃去其重複之字，縮摹之，以餉學者。”《縮摹泰山石經峪字》第248－249頁。
以原大複製的刻本和縮印的石印本出現於20世紀初。涉川玄耳第44頁。石印本有兩個優點：其一，相比於大尺寸紙張之上的原拓本，此種複製品更易於處理；其二，兩洋元的售價相對便宜。
第一部墨拓《金剛經》的縮印本由震亞圖書局於1915年12月在《申報》廣而告之，并於次年出版發行。《介紹碑帖》，見《申報》1915年12月28日，第11版。此書116頁，每頁12字，計1392字。《泰山經石峪金剛經墨拓》。此書銷量甚佳，而其他縮印版本亦是如此，吸引着民國時期書法家與學者。
不過，這些流行版本的可靠性經常遭受質疑。漫漶的刻字有時會被其他現存的拓本替代，而那些至今可見的刻字，如刻經起始的“佛説”二字，未見於這些版本。見下文。
民國時期出版的僞本，有一套由王大錯（活動於1910－1935年）製作的拓本。此拓本包括拓片1002張，另有一篇明顯托名名家劉墉（1719－1805）的序，此序將此拓本定爲不晚於金朝或元朝。此書影印本出版於1984年。見本卷“徵引資料”之“拓本”條所列拓本K，。邵陽市圖書館藏《明拓孤本泰山金剛經》乃另一套僞本。見本卷“徵引資料”之“拓本”條所列，。

figure.head: Couplet with characters culled from the Sutra Stone Valley Diamond Sutra at the inner entrance of Stone Drum Academy in Hengyang City, Hunan. Photograph taken in 1922; after 3, fig. III-102
《金剛經》摩崖集字對聯，位於湖南衡陽市石鼓書院二門。1922年拍攝，采自常盤大定和關野貞第3頁，圖III-102

div.head: History of Rubbings, Outline Drawings, and Reproductions
div.p: Before the Republican period, rubbings of the Diamond Sutra were hard to come by. In the early seventeenth century, a travel companion of Bi Zi­yan 畢自嚴 (1569–1638) once ordered his assistants to make rubbings of nine characters for his personal enjoyment. 446a. See Sources, p. . As mentioned earlier, at the end of the eighteenth century, 296 rubbings were available to Ruan Yuan.See above, note .

During the second half of the nineteenth century, the number of characters in complete sets increased significantly thanks to scholarly efforts at the site. Sets of rubbings with more than 900 characters had become available, when the removal of debris made it possible to see that many characters for the first time.See Sources, Li Zuoxian, p. .

In his diary, the scholar Jiang Biao 江標 (1860–1899) mentions reading Ruan Yuan’s notebook in the spring of 1884He refers to Ruan Yuan’s Jottings from the Minor Pavilion at the Azure Waves (Xiaocanglang bitan 小滄浪筆談), 3: 40b–41a; see Sources, Ruan Yuan, p. . and then meeting an old rubbing seller in Tai’an who had a set of more than 800 characters, a number—Jiang noted—strikingly different from Ruan Yuan’s count of 296. 1: 24. In the fall of the same year Jiang ascended Mount Tai and visited the Diamond Sutra, where he counted more than 950 characters. 1: 44. Here he refers to 14483–14484; see Sources, Ruan Yuan, p. . In 1899 when he came to Tai’an again, he bought a set of more than 950 characters for ten thousand cash. 1: 344.

At the time Jiang Biao bought his set in Tai’an, eminent scholar-officials had difficulties acquiring rubbings outside the city. Luo Zhenyu 羅振玉 (1866–1940) claimed to possess a letter from Pan Zu­yin 潘祖蔭‎ (1830–1890), a powerful official and leading figure in antiquarian circles, asking his friend Chen Jie­qi 陳介祺 (1813–1884) to search for a complete set of rubbings. After much effort, Luo himself acquired what he thought was a complete set in 1897.
475; see Sources, Luo Zhenyu, p. . Ma Heng still said in the 1920s about cliff inscriptions in general, that they are “deep in the mountains and in secluded valleys, and that it is difficult to tap rubbings. This is why complete versions are most difficult to obtain. 深山窮谷，難與椎拓，故完全之本至不易致。” (84).

Luo was also closely associated with the first Japanese scholars to study the sutra. In 1902 Yamamoto Kyōzan 山本竟山 (1863–1934) became a student of Luo through the introduction of the elder Kusakabe Meikaku 日下部鳴鶴 (1838–1922). 253. Yamamoto went to China at least seven times. When in 1903 Kusakabe thanked Yamamoto for bringing him rubbings of the “large characters of Mount Tai 泰山大字,” he already had visited Luo twice.For the letter exchange of Kusakabe and Yamamoto, see 86–88. Kusakabe probably used a selection of these very rubbings and illustrated them in his treatise Discourse on Calligraphy (Shodan 書談), 21–22.

In the Republican period, the availability of rubbings of the Diamond Sutra increased greatly, in spite of an official prohibition, not strictly enforced, against making them.See above, p. . In 1946 Li Dongchen vividly described the business of making rubbings:
div.quote:
For those in town who are in the business of rubbings and models for calligraphy, the mass-produced commodity are the rubbings of Sutra Stone Valley. In spring or autumn on good days the rubbing makers went there, while during summer rain and winter frost they stopped working one after another. In recent years the business has decayed and almost nobody shows interest. The rubbings are one character per sheet, of which there are three kinds: complete sets, sets without duplicate characters, and characters adjoined in couplets or phrases. There are two methods of making rubbings: black and red rubbings. For the black rubbings they use soot ink, for red rubbings they use cinnabar or fuchsin and egg white. Because the mountain gales are the harshest in the valley, they only make the outlines of the characters on paper, and, after returning home, correct them again and supplement the surrounding parts. Those who can make more than a hundred rubbings in one day are called skilled hands. 邑中業碑帖者，大宗交易品爲經石峪拓片。春秋佳日，椎拓者往；夏雨冬霜，相率停工。近年事業彫零，几無問津者。拓片一紙一字，分爲三種，曰全套，曰不重字，曰集聯集句。拓法有墨拓，有朱拓。墨拓用𤇆墨爲之，朱拓用銀硃或品紅和蛋白爲之。因山風最厲在峪，衹拓字口，歸而重行修正，補拓周圍。一日能成百餘字者，曰能手。
6a–b.
div.p: Here we learn that rubbing makers produced different standardized sets for the market and that there were great differences in quality among them. Most rubbings of single characters were sold by antique dealers. Fu Zhenlun wrote in 1931 that rubbings were available everywhere in the streets of Tai’an, 517. and in a travel guide of 1936 rubbings of the Diamond Sutra are listed among the most popular souvenirs in every bookshop in the street Ximen dajie 西門大街. 17–18. Even antique dealers in distant provinces kept rubbings from the Diamond Sutra in stock. In a 1921 catalogue of the Xiling Seal Engraver’s Society (Xiling yinshe 西泠印社) in Hangzhou,complete sets of rubbings were sold for 30 silver dollars, sets without repeated characters for 10, and four- or five-character couplets for 4 and 6 jiao 角 (1/10 silver dollar). 625. The society was established in 1904 with the famous Wu Changshuo 吴昌碩 (1844–1927) as its director.

Single character rubbings rubbings were collected as calligraphic models already in the late eighteenth century.Jin Qi 金棨 (fl. 1794–1808) has collected rubbings and used them as calligraphic models to write a wooden plaque for a Daoist temple hall in Tai’an in 1797. See 64; 1126–1127. They became popular souvenirs and gifts in the early nineteenth century.Xu Zonggan 徐宗幹 (1796–1866) noted that the Magistrate of Qufu, Wang Dahuai 王大淮 (1785–1844), once gave him auspicious characters from Stone Sutra Valley, which he believed Wang obtained while serving in the office of the Mongol official Songyun 松筠 (1752–1835). See 335; for Songyun’s career, see 691–692. Li Ciming 李慈銘 (1830–1894) reported in a diary entry of 1890 that he acquired four boxes of donkey-hide gelatin, dried pork, Jujube, and a couplet assembled with characters of the Diamond Sutra from a visitor. 17: 12627. Later accounts show that they were usually acquired in sets of four or eight, assembled as short sayings. For example the architectural historian Sekino Tadashi 關野貞 (1867–1935) owned rubbings of eight characters, which he probably acquired during his fieldwork in Shandong in 1907. They formed a four-character couplet of Confucian content: “Know the ordinances and rejoice in heaven; cultivate virtue and sow rightousness. 知命樂天，樹德種義。” The first half, a quote from the Book of Changes (Yi jing 易經) was a tribute to his father, Sekino Takatoki 關野峻節, who had the sobriquet Rakuten 樂天.Cf. 1: 77c; for the reference to his father, see The second half he presented soon after to the Buddhologist Tokiwa Daijō 常盤大定 (1870–1945).On their relationship, see The Historiography of Sutra Stone Valley in the present volume, part 1, p. . The linguist and calligrapher Gotō Asatarō 後藤朝太郎 (1881–1945) acquired some rubbings during his forays through the Beijing market places in 1925. For his friend, the artist and businessman Ibuki Shin 伊吹震 (1888–1961), he chose three rubbings reading “Old Taiju” (Rō Taiju 老大樹), politely addressing him with the latter’s artist name. 248. For Gotō’s prolific career, see 200–208.

figure.head: The First Meeting of the Ninth Society: relatives, disciples, and other persons concerned at the Sōseki Sanbō, former home of the deceased Natsume Sōseki, on January 9, 1917. Photograph, 11 × 15.8 cm; courtesy of Kanagawa Museum of Modern Literature, Yokohama (No. 720/150)
1917年1月9日在夏目漱石故居漱石山房參加第一回九日會的夏目氏親屬、門生和相關人士。照片長11厘米，寬15.8厘米。日本縣立神奈川近代文學舘藏品（編號No.720/150）

div.p: Rubbings were objects for aesthetic appreciation, suitable as decorations in many different settings. Couplets were the most popular format. They adorned private homes and studios, as well as the meeting halls of political and religious organizations, in both China and Japan. A couplet with a Confucian maxim consisting of characters culled from the sutra framed the inner entrance of Stone Drum Academy (Shi­gu shu­yuan 石鼓書院) in Heng­yang City 衡陽市, Hu­nan province (fig. ): “When cultivating a reputation atop a thousand Buddhas, the utmost flavor is in the Five Classics. 修名千佛上，至味五經中。” It was destroyed during the war in 1944, and it is not known when the couplet was installed, but the public display of characters from the Sutra Stone Valley Diamond Sutra had already begun in the mid nineteenth century. When the official Zhang Xianghe 張祥河 (1785–1862) was Governor of Shaanxi and supervised the provincial examinations of 1849 in Qinzhong, he installed the same maxim also with characters culled from the Sutra Stone Valley Diamond Sutra in the examination hall.Zhang refers to this episode in his poem titled Inspired by the Autumn Examination of the renzi year (Ren­zi qiu­wei ji­shi 壬子秋闈即事), annotating the line “A thousand Buddhas caress the characters of Sutra Valley. 千佛摩挲經峪字。” See 540. The maxim was probably carved on wooden boards, which were then hung on the wall.A rubbing of the characters was on sale in an auction of 2017 (https://web.artfoxlive.com/product/673357.html, accessed August 3, 2021). The colophon left and right of the couplet reads: “Assembled from the Cliff Inscription at Mount Tai Sutra Stone Valley by a Northern Qi man, hung in the Hall of Utmost Veneration in Qinzhong 秦中. Written during the 1849 examination at county level by its supervisor, Governor of Shaanxi province, Zhang Xianghe from Huating. 集泰山經石峪北齊人摩崖懸秦中至公堂。道光已酉(1849)科鄉監臨陝西巡撫華亭張祥河識。” The official Pan Yan­tong 潘衍桐 (1841–1874) had a board with the characters nan­yuan 南園 in his guest hall.The official Shao Rilian 邵日濂 (1854–1929) referred to them in his poem Visiting my fellow graduate Pan Yiqin (Fang Pan Yiqin tongnian 訪潘嶧琴同年). See 1687: 42. While in the nineteenth century the reports are scarce, it seems to have become a widespread trend in the Republican Period.Shibukawa Genji observed in his travel report of 1916, that such single characters mounted as couplets could often be seen adorning salons or studies (12). See Sources, Shibukawa Genji, p. . The China for Christ Movement 中華歸主 hung rubbings of the four characters of their name and lithographic reproductions as horizontal and vertical signs on the building of their Shanghai branch, as reported in 1922 (189).

In the Waseda University Aizu Museum the four-character phrase, Masterpieces and Great Works (Meicho Taisaku 名著大作), with rubbings mounted in the format of a horizontal placard, was recently reinstalled at its original place above the entrance of the hall that formerly housed the stacks of Waseda University Library 早稲田圖書館.Illustrated and described in 275. For a historical and a current photograph of the placard in its place, see https://www.waseda.jp/culture/aizu-museum/news/2017/10/17/1867/ (accessed September 24, 2021). The first head of the library, the antiquarian Ichishima Shunjō 市島春城 (1860–1944), selected the four characters from rubbings that Matsudaira Yasukuni 松平康國 (1863–1945) acquired in China and donated to the university in 1904.Matsudaira went to China after he had received an appointment as translator by Yuan Shikai 袁世凯, at that time Viceroy of Zhili 直隸總督. The acquisition of the rubbings is reported in his travelogue. See 34; Sources, Matsudaira Yasukuni, p. . Fig. shows a gathering in the former home of the famous writer and professor at the Faculty of Letters of Tōkyō Imperial University, Natsume Sōseki 夏目漱石 (1867–1916), one month after his death in December 1916. Members of his family, friends, distinguished intellectuals and colleagues met in the Sōseki sanbō 漱石山房 to talk about his last months and his legacy. The room was decorated with rubbings of the Diamond Sutra mounted on a folding screen.For the names of the persons on this photograph, see 7. For this photograph and another one in which the same screen is also visible, see figs. 101 and 102. A replica of the screen is now in the rebuilt study at the Natsume Sōseki Memorial Museum 漱石山房記念館, Tōkyō. Several visitors to his home noted the prominently displayed rubbings, which reflects the prestigious standing and fame of the calligraphy among the highest cultural circles in Japan.Several writers mentioned the rubbings. Akutagawa Ryūnosuke 芥川龍之介 (1892–1927) who frequently attended the literary gatherings at the home of his idol recounts one screen, and according to Matsuoka Yuzuru 松岡譲 (1891–1969) there were two screens: one at the entrance and one in the study. See 386 and 487, and 278–279. All these examples show quite well the use of characters in monumental calligraphy.The role of the Diamond Sutra in the discourse on monumental calligraphy is discussed in the article The Historiography of Sutra Stone Valley in the present volume, part 1, p. .

Visitors also approached the rubbing makers in the valley directly. In 1922 a member of a party of Japanese visitors to Sutra Stone Valley searched for auspicious characters such as “long life” (ju 壽), “happiness” (fuku 福), or the well-known phrase “perfect happiness and prosperity” (fukutoku enman 福德圓滿) and had rubbings of them made on the spot. 485–486. See also Sources, Shibukawa Genji, p. . Such practices were also popular among Chinese visitors. While teaching in Nanjing at National Central University 國立中央大學, the famous painter Xu Beihong 徐悲鴻 (1895–1953), a student of Kang You­wei, took a colleague and a class of fourteen students on a graduation trip to Mount Tai in 1931. Xu was certainly familiar with his teacher’s admiration for the Diamond Sutra. When the group rested at Sutra Stone Valley, Xu could scarcely tear himself away from examining the characters when it was time to leave. . He bought rubbings of eight that he had selected to form the couplet “With individual views and self-willed actions. 獨特偏見，一意孤行。” .

The provenance of the many rubbings distributed all over the world is usually unclear, but in one instance it is traceable. A set which the great buddhologist Tokiwa Daijō 常盤大定 (1870–1945) brought back from Shandong is partly preserved in Japan. Tokiwa visited Sutra Stone Valley on October 11, 1921 and had the rubbings shipped to Tōkyō a few days later. 218–219. Most of his rubbings, however, went up in flames in the Great Kantō earthquake 關東大震灾 of September 1, 1923 and later in the Second World War. 40–41, and 12. Yet 102 rubbings of single characters have survived. He donated them to the Shūren Temple 宗蓮寺 in Mikawa Town 三川町, Yamagata Prefecture 山形縣. Chiyo 千代, the daughter of its head priest Honda Zōkai 本多藏界 (1843–1918), was his wife. . Ninety rubbings have been pasted into the coffered ceiling of the main hall and twelve rubbings above the sliding doors of the reception room (fig. ). The rubbings in the ceiling point in different directions and do not form a single text. Yet unlike in most displays of Diamond Sutra rubbings the characters are here seen in a Buddhist environment.

figure.head: Rubbings donated by Tokiwa Daijō mounted in the main hall and reception hall of Shūrenji 宗蓮寺 in Mikawa Town 三川町, Yamagata Prefecture 山形縣. Photographs taken in 2021; courtesy of Shūrenji, Yokokawa
常盤大定捐贈的《金剛經》拓片，張貼於日本山形縣三川町宗蓮寺本堂和客間欄間。2021年拍攝，宗蓮寺藏品

div.p: While rubbings promoted admiration for the calligraphy of the sutra, they also disseminated inaccurate visual information. Few were made for serious research by epigraphers, and rubbing makers did not hesitate to enhance their products through a variety of clever but deceptive techniques. These included intensifying the darkness of the background ink after a rubbing was made to set off the shapes of the carved lines more sharply. 42.

The act of making rubbings may seem to be a purely mechanical means of reproducing inscriptions, yet visual information was actually lost by transferring the three-dimensional shape of the engraved stone into a two dimensional medium.

A photograph of three Chinese rubbing makers at work taken in 1929 shows how these men simply traced the outlines of characters, as observed by Li Dongchen, later blackening the surrounding paper to create the effect of an actual rubbing (fig. ). The same photograph shows the characters that were recut on the vertical stone wall from which rubbings could be taken (JSY 58). Some rubbing makers are said to have re-cut the characters on plaster walls in their homes, saving themselves the effort of repeated trips to the Valley. But what they produced could only be inferior versions. 84; 393; 83. For a comparison of two rubbings of the same character, see 55–57. Even today characters have been copied in original size on a large piece of rock at Dai Temple (fig. ) and on rows of stone slabs set into a wall of one of its courtyards (fig. ).

figure.head: Part of Sutra Stone Valley (JSY 1, JSY 12, and JSY 22) recreated at Dai Temple; photograph taken 2008
岱廟一院落中重刻的經石峪《金剛經》（經石峪1號）、經石峪12號和經石峪 22號刻銘。2008年拍攝

div.p: Rubbing makers also made deliberate distortions. For example, in order to achieve a unified size of characters that were intended to be pinned on columns of buildings, they intentionally shortened or extended strokes, as Sun Baotian relates. Because rubbings of single characters do not preserve the spatial relation of the carved characters, and because single sheets have no unique identifiers, their original order is easily confused. To avoid this problem, Sun commissioned a rubbing of the title and the entire first 29 text columns on one large sheet of paper.See Sources, Sun Baotian, p. . For his account on the sutra on Mount Ge (Ge 1; 217–218) Sun also relied on rubbings of entire columns. See 9254–9255; translated in 144.

All this shows that, although rubbings are an indispensable tool for study of the Diamond Sutra, they must be used with caution. They document how the appearance of characters may have changed over time and reveal that rubbing makers “doctored” the rubbings by altering the shapes of the brushstrokes or even invented characters.

In addition to rubbings, the technique known as “double outlines” (shuanggou 雙鈎), already practiced in the Tang dynasty and often used to copy works of calligraphy, was employed to reproduce the carved sutra characters.See 34–35. The technique was described by Jiang Kui 姜夔 (1155–1221) in the section Copying and Tracing (linmo 臨摹) in his Sequel to the Manual on Calligraphy (Xu shupu 續書譜), written in 1208. 300–301. Yang Shoujing traced the outlines of the strokes of characters from the Diamond Sutra without taking the final step of filling in the outlines with ink. His outline reproductions were published in 1907 as Characters of Mount Tai Diamond Sutra in Reduced Size (Suo­mo Tai Shan Shijing Yu zi 縮摹泰山石經峪字) (fig. ). The copyright page of this edition is dated 1907, with a preface dated 1909. Yang also reproduced the Stone Hymn (Tie 2) in this technique. See 27. The contemporary experts on stone inscriptions in Shandong, Ouyang Zhongshi 歐陽中石 and An Tingshan 安廷山 still praised the precision of Yang’s drawings in their 1986 introduction to his work. In his preface Yang explains that his goal was to find a handy format that allowed him to reproduce the shapes of the characters for study by scholars and connoisseurs of calligraphy. 248–249.

figure.head: Copies of characters from the Sutra Stone Valley Diamond Sutra in a courtyard of Dai Temple; photograph taken 2006
岱廟院落中摹刻的經石峪《金剛經》部分刻字。2006年拍攝

div.p: Rubbings reproduced as full-sized woodblock prints and as reduced size lithographic reproductions also were available in the early twentieth century. 44. The latter had two advantages: they were easier to handle than the large sheets of the original rubbings, and at a list price of two silver dollars (yangyuan 洋元) they were considerably cheaper.

The first book with reproductions of reduced-size rubbings of the Diamond Sutra was announced by the Zhenya Bookstore 震亞圖書局 in the Shanghai News (Shenbao 申報) in December 1915 and published in 1916.“Introducing Calligraphic Model Books of Stele Inscriptions (Jieshao bei tie 介紹碑帖).” (Shenbao 申報), December 28, 1915: 11. This publication of 116 pages illustrates 12 characters per page for a total of 1,392. .
The book sold well, and so did other editions of reduced-size rubbings throughout the Republican period, appealing to both calligraphers and scholars.

The reliability of these popular editions was often questionable. Effaced characters sometimes were replaced by rubbings of others still extant, while characters of the sutra that remain clearly visible today, including the first two, foshuo 佛説, do not appear.See below, p. .

Among the numerous spurious sets of rubbings that have been published since the Republican period was that by Wang Dacuo 王大錯 (fl. 1910–1935), consisting of 1,002 rubbings and an apparently forged preface by the famous artist Liu Yong 劉墉 (1719–1805), which dated the rubbings not later than the Jin 金 (1115–1234) or Yuan 元 dynasty (1271–1368).A facsimile copy of the original book was published in 1984. See Reproductions, p. , set K. Another spurious set in the Shaoyang Municipal Library 邵陽市圖書館, Hunan province, is titled The Only Existing Version of the Diamond Sutra at Mount Tai that was Rubbed in the Ming Dynasty [1368–1644] 明拓孤本泰山金剛經. See Reproductions, p. , set I.

figure.head: Outlines of characters ru 如 (2/1) and shi 是 (2/2) traced by Yang Shoujing; after 253
楊守敬縮摹的“如”（2/1）和“是”（2/2）二字。采自《縮摹泰山石經峪字》第253頁
Lai Fei making a rubbing of the character jing 經（22/20） in 2006
2006年學者賴非製作“經”字（22/20）拓本

div.head: 現代複製品
div.p: 20世紀80年代以降，經石峪《金剛經》刻字複製精確度取得長足進步，原因之一是采用了攝影技術。2003年，安廷山出版了所有刻字的黑白照片，而這些刻字剛被描紅；同時，他在此書中也采用雙鈎複製。在本卷中，我們首次刊布了刻經址全貌的彩色照片。

不僅如此，刻經文字存檔記録也變得更加可靠，如表格所示“大”字：竪行均爲“大”字，但每個字出自刻經的不同位置。横行則以年代降序排列不同複製品，最上方一行是諸刻字極爲精確的彩色照片。而不確定性不可避免，即使相隔數年拍攝的照片之間，也顯然有别。項目研究組於2006年實地考察所得2/19、3/40、31/58填紅諸字與安廷山2003年發表的拓本C有所不同。安廷山此書所收雙鈎本與1907年楊守敬刻本對比表明，雙鈎祇能説明刻字的輪廓。拓本A、B、C、D、E均拓製於近數十年，藏於濟南山東省石刻藝術博物館和泰安市博物館；拓本F、G、H、I、J、K則爲民國時期乃至更早之作。這些早期拓本往往會使拓片之外的底墨看起來更加均匀。而這種均匀性在拓本I、H、K中被誇大，付諸石印時灰色陰影完全消失。近期的拓片趨於更加精確。一個典型例子便是含有“大”字 (31/58) 的最後一列，此字僅刊刻了輪廓。除了一套早期拓本之外，幾乎所有的拓本忽視了這個字，而拓本H中的此字筆畫似乎被完整刊刻。然而，研究那些隨着時間推移而損毁的刻字時，早期拓片仍然有用。此字的右下角在拓本A、C和D中均不可見，而在拓本F至拓本K中可見其不儘相同的全貌，儘管其形狀各异。這再次表明，應該慎用複製品。

figure.head: Stone step that existed at the time of carving in the lower parts of columns 4 to 7; photograph taken in 2006
第4至第7行下部的石階保留了刻經伊始的原狀。2006年拍攝

div.head: Modern Reproductions
div.p: From the 1980s onward the accuracy in the reproduction of the Diamond Sutra characters has made great progress. A big step forward was the introduction of photography. In 2003 An Tingshan published black and white photographs of all characters whose shapes had recently been heightened with a red pigment. In the same book he also used again the old double outline technique. In the present volume we present for the first time color photographs of the entire stone surface.

Advances have been made also in the production of rubbings.For important sets of rubbings and details of their publication, see Reproductions, in the catalog section below, p. . The rubbing editions are also listed in , , , and . No rubbings of the Diamond Sutra have been made for commercial purposes since the founding of the People’s Republic of China. Yet on at least five occasions complete sets have been produced for scholarly research. One set was made in 1965 when the new administrative structures for cultural heritage protection and tourism were established. In the table with sample characters, fig. , and in the catalog, p. , it is set E. In the summer of 1991, Lai Fei and a team of researchers from the Shandong Stone Carving Art Museum spent a month at Sutra Stone Valley producing a new set of rubbings, published in 1992 (set D). In the early 2000s the local authorities commissioned set B, and in 2003 An Tingshan reproduced photographs, rubbings and outline drawings, sets C-1, C-2, and C-3. In the present volume we illustrate an hitherto unpublished set A, supplemented by rubbings of set D and one character of set E (fig. ). Set B: zhuo 著 (4/12), gong 恭 (4/16), yi 一 (7/36), sheng 生 (7/39), zhi 之 (9/2), mie 滅 (9/20), yi 以 (9/24), pu 菩 (9/27), ti 提 (9/28), ruo 若 (9/29), bu 不 (10/26), shi 施 (10/30), fa 法 (10/37), bu 布 (10/38), sa 薩 (12/35), wu 无 (12/36), zhu 住 (12/37), shen 身 (13/42), xu 須 (14/18), fan 凡 (14/21), xin 心 (15/44), zhuo 著 (17/49), qu 取 (18/6), ji 即 (18/9), yi 以 (18/44), bi 比 (19/2), qiu 丘 (19/3), zhi 知 (19/4), jie 解 (19/52), ren 人 (21/33), san 三 (23/1), yi 意 (23/28), pu 菩 (25/22), ti 提 (25/23), han 含 (25/30), neng 能 (25/31), ye 也 (25/47), he 何 (25/50), yi 以 (25/51), ming 名 (26/55), dao 道 (28/10), shi 世 (28/11), zun 尊 (28/12), yun 云 (28/52), he 何 (28/53), ru 如 (28/54), fo 佛 (28/60), suo 所 (28/61), zhuang 莊 (29/30), yan 嚴 (29/31), fo 佛 (29/32), shi 是 (29/55), ying 應 (29/74), se 色 (29/76), sheng 生 (29/77), wang 王 (30/30), yu 扵 (30/31), yi 意 (30/32), yun 云 (30/33), shen 身 (30/54), you 有 (30/67), sha 沙 (31/28), xu 須 (31/29), ti 提 (31/31), wo 我 (31/32), jin 今 (31/33), shi 實 (31/34), yan 言 (31/35), gao 告 (31/36), shi 施 (31/65), fu 福 (32/28), de 徳 (32/29), fu 復 (32/34), xu 須 (32/36), pu 菩 (32/37), er 尒 (33/33), sa 卅 (35/41), er 二 (35/42), ji 即 (35/44), shi 是 (35/45), fei 非 (35/46), xiang 相 (35/47), shi ming 名 (35/49), sa 卅 (35/50), yi 以 (35/64), huan 洹 (35/65), he 河 (35/66), sha 沙 (35/67), ruo 若 (35/73), fu 復 (35/74), jing 經 (35/79), zhong 中 (35/80), shou 壽 (39/38), shi 時 (39/51), ruo 若 (39/52), wo 我 (39/54), xiang 相 (39/55), ren 人 (39/56), xiang 相 (39/57), pu 菩 (40/14), sheng 聲 (40/43), zhu 住 (40/54), ruo 若 (40/56), you 有 (40/58), bu 不 (41/73), de 徳 (42/50); set E: ming 明 (41/86).

The documentation also has become more reliable, as a table with examples of the character da 大 from the sutra shows (fig. ). One vertical column shows always the same character da on the rock, yet in different forms of reproduction; in each column the character comes from a different location within the sutra. The horizontal rows are different reproductions arranged in descending chronological order. The uppermost row with the color photographs contains the most precise images of the characters. Yet uncertainties remain. Even between photographs taken only a few years apart, differences are conspicuous. The red paint of the characters 2/19, 3/40, and 31/58 was different during the fieldwork of the research group in 2006 from that in set C published by An Tingshan in 2003. The comparison of outline drawings C by An Tingshan of 2003 and those by Yang Shoujing of 1907 shows that even this technique can only give an interpretation of the shape.

The rubbing sets A, B, C-2, D, and E were all made in recent decades and are kept in collections in Jinan and Tai’an. Sets F, G, H, I, J, and K, date to the Republican Period or before. These older rubbings tend to make the surrounding black background appear more homogenous. This uniformity is exaggerated in the versions I, H and K, in which the transfer to lithographic printing caused the complete loss of shades of grey. Recent rubbings tend to be more precise. A case in point is the last column with the character da (31/58), which was carved only in outlines. All but one of the early sets omitted this character, and in set H the character is shown as if the strokes were carved in full. Nevertheless older rubbings are still useful when studying characters that have been damaged over the course of time. The lower right part of the character is not visible in sets A, C, and D. The stroke is visible in its entirety in sets F to K, though in a considerable variety of shapes, which shows once again that one needs to use reproductions with caution.

figure.head: Characters pu 菩 (39/11), shi 是 (40/11), gu 故 (40/12), and fei 非 (41/12) carved in outline on a quartz vein; photograph taken in 2006
在石英帶面雙鈎刊刻的“菩”(39/11)、“是”(40/11)、“故”(40/12)和“非”(41/12)字。2006年拍攝
The six extant examples of the character da 大 and different reproductions. For details, see list below, pp.
不同複製品中現存的6個“大”字。詳情見下文，列表

div.head: 内容
div.p: 泰山經石峪所刻經文乃鳩摩羅什（*Kumā­rajīva，344－413）完成於402年的《金剛經》漢譯本前半部分。《金剛般若波羅蜜經》T#235, 8：748c16－752c7。 此經成書紀年不詳，學界認爲撰於公元2世紀或4世紀。蕭平第227頁。《金剛經》是極具影響力的大乘佛經之一，具有豐富内涵與禮儀功能。這部重要文獻闡明了空的教義，却未提及術語“空” （ śūnyatā ）這一般若經典（ prajñāpāramitā ）的基本概念。信徒深信，《金剛經》具有療愈和救贖的功能，并含有儀式使用的明確指示及如何廣爲傳播的諄諄善導。遵循着這些指示的信衆對經文所作解説和抄寫難以計數。大量抄寫此經的最早例證是紀年南朝梁大同元年正月一日（535年2月18日）的一件殘卷，比經石峪《金剛經》的刊刻時間早幾十年。一位南梁使節淳于氏在茹茹國發願敬造《金剛經》一百份，并在安然回到梁朝後完成心願。唐長孺第192－193頁過録并討論了淳于氏的題記。許雲和認爲此百份抄本完成於茹茹國。然題記殘缺，許氏的這一解讀佐證不足。通過抄經，淳于氏求取功德，并傳播佛教信仰。這些抄本尚存一件殘卷（）。《中村不折旧藏》第142－143頁。
刊刻在經石峪的《金剛經》可分成三部分：引言、教義和結語。第一部分以給孤獨長者布施的祇樹給孤獨園中的精舍日常儀式開篇。一千二百五十名大比丘在佛陀的帶領下，着衣持缽，進入舍衛城化緣。食訖，他們有序地脱去袍子，放下托缽，洗足，敷座而坐（第2、3行）。這種莊嚴儀式化寺院生活引入其後關於“心”這一話題的生動論述。

第二部分即主體部分，由佛陀及其弟子須菩提的問答組成。諸弟子入席就座後，須菩提提出一個基本問題，也是諸多開始修行佛法的信徒提出的問題： “世尊！善男子、善女人，發阿耨多羅三藐三菩提心，應云何住？云何降伏其心？”（第5行），《金剛般若波羅蜜經》T#235，8：748c27－29。而佛陀驚人地否定自己的福德與功績，回應道：“所有一切衆生之類……我皆令入無餘涅槃而滅度之。如是滅度無量、無數、無邊衆生，實無衆生得滅度者。”（第8、9行）《金剛般若波羅蜜經》T#235，8：749a6－10。 緊接着，佛陀繼續闡釋這一論點：“若菩薩有我相、人相、衆生相、壽者相，“相”，或作“想”。左冠明 令人信服地論證 “相”這一術語在鳩摩羅什譯本中應讀作“想”。即非菩薩。” (第9、10 行）。此段落梵文本的英譯，見何離巽第143頁。對鳩摩羅什漢文譯本的述評，見何離巽。佛陀在此告誡，菩薩若執着於任何“相”，即無從降伏其心。
此外，佛陀指出，菩薩行布施“於法應無所住”。（第10行）《金剛般若波羅蜜經》T#235，8：749a12。因此，刻經在第10行與第11行之間的第一處“通道”之前，已將 “無住”與“無著”等重要概念闡述完畢 。

佛陀繼續與須菩提對話，反復闡發：“凡所有相，皆是虚妄。若見諸相非相，則見如來。”（第14行）《金剛般若波羅蜜經》T#235，8：749a24－25。佛陀説，持戒修行者“不應取法”（第18行），《金剛般若波羅蜜經》T#235, 8：749b9。因爲“説法，如栰喻者，“木筏譬喻”意指，佛陀的教義就像渡河後不再需要的木筏。此譬喻上溯巴利經藏《中部》第一部分第134－135段，其英譯見霍納第173－174頁。此譬喻又見於漢譯經典《中阿含經》T#26（200），1：764b18－c14，以及《增壹阿含經》T#125（43.5），2：759c29－760a26。有關此句的精闢研究，見宗玉媺。法尚應捨，何况非法。” （第19行）《金剛般若波羅蜜經》T#235，8：749b11。佛陀隨後解釋道，菩薩“應無所住而生其心。”（第30行）《金剛般若波羅蜜經》T#235，8：749c22－23。 此處佛陀重復同韵律節奏的固定論述模式，也就是注疏家有關《金剛經》漢文著書的綜述，見馬毅。所言“三句”，即“某某，即非某某，是名某某”。例如：“如來説卅二相，即是非相，是名卅二相。”（第35行）《金剛般若波羅蜜經》T#235，8：750a22－23。又如：“如來説第一波羅蜜，非第一波羅蜜，是名第一波羅蜜。”（第38行）《金剛般若波羅蜜經》T#235，8：750b12－13。這些看似矛盾的主張再次强調修行者不應“着相”，因爲諸相没有本我自性，皆爲虚空。這種對諸相的否定以一個本生故事達到極致，在這個故事中，佛陀在一前世爲王時，逐塊布施全身。
第二部分的對話以佛陀揭示信衆所奉持此經的經題而結束。儘管前文一再强調講解説法的重要性，還藉由須菩提之口提醒： “如来无所説法。”（第34行）所有現象的空性，乃至佛陀的話語（即木筏），再次被恰到好處地强調。
第三部分，即最後一部分，贊頌大乘佛經的功德。抄經、奉經誦經勝過任何布施，甚至勝過本生故事揭示的肉身犧牲，此種修行被視作“小法”。刻經末句作出清晰且明確的指示：此經應該如同佛身舍利，通過繞行儀式以獲得最高的崇敬禮拜。有關佛經作爲塔廟之崇拜，見本卷上册《此經是塔》一文。

div.head: Content
div.p:
Carved in Sutra Stone Valley on Mount Tai is about the first half of the Diamond Sutra
in the translation into Chinese completed by *Kumā­rajīva (鳩摩羅什; 344–413) in 402.T#235, 8: 748c16–752c7. The date of composition is unclear, but scholars believe that this sutra was composed in the second or the fourth century of the Common Era. 227. The Diamond Sutra is one of the most influential Mahayana scriptures, a seminal text with multiple layers of meaning and ritual functions. It illustrates the doctrine of emptiness, without, however, mentioning the term śūnyatā (kong 空), a fundamental concept of prajñāpāramitā literature. The sutra is believed to have healing and salvific properties, and contains clear advice for its own ritual use and instructions on how it should be disseminated. Devotees who followed these instructions have expounded and copied it incalculable times. The earliest known instance of a mass reproduction of the sutra is dated to the first day of the era datong of the Southern Liang dynasty 南朝梁大同元年正月一日 (February 18, 535), some decades before it was carved in Sutra Stone Valley. While in the Rouran kingdom, an envoy of the Southern Liang dynasty surnamed Chunyu 淳于 vowed to have 100 copies written. Upon his safe return, he fulfilled his vow. 192–193 transcribes and discusses Chuanyu’s colophon. suggests that the 100 copies were written in Turfan. Since some keywords in the colophon are missing, there is not enough evidence to support his reading. In so doing, he gained merit and disseminated the Buddhist tradition. Only fragments of one of these copies have survived (fig. ). 142–143.

The Diamond Sutra carved in Sutra Stone Valley can be neatly divided into three parts: introduction, doctrinal teaching, and conclusion. The first part opens with the daily ritual in the monastery in the Jeta Grove, which was donated by Anāthapiṇḍika. An assembly of 1250 great monks under the leadership of the Buddha don their robes, take their alms bowls, and go to the city of Śrāvastī for alms. Having eaten, they take off their robes, put down their alms bowls in an orderly fashion, wash their feet, and all take a seat (columns 2–3). This solemn, ritualized procedure of life in the monastery introduces the vivid discourse on the issues of the mind that follows.

The second and main part of the carved text contains an exchange of questions and answers between the Buddha and his disciple Subhūti. After the assembly is seated, Subhūti poses a basic question asked by many who set out on the Buddhist path: “World-Honored One, how should a son or daughter of a good family who has conceived of an aspiration for supreme perfect awakening ( anuttarāṃ samyak-saṃbodhim abhi­saṃ­buddhāḥ ) abide? How should they tame their mind? 世尊！善男子、善女人，發阿耨多羅三藐三菩提心，應云何住？云何降伏其心？” (column 5).T#235, 8: 748c27–29. The Buddha replies, in the process astonishing his audience by denying his own merits and achievements: “All kinds of beings . . . I make all of them enter ‘nirvana without remainder’ and bring them to pass over into extinction. In this way, I have brought incalculable, innumerable, unlimited beings to pass over into extinction, yet in reality, there is no being that has passed over into extinction. 所有一切衆生之類……我皆令入無餘涅槃而滅度之。如是滅度無量、無數、無邊衆生，實無衆生得滅度者。” (columns 8 and 9).T#235, 8: 749a6–10. Without delay, the Buddha then explains the reasons for his argument: “If, for a Bodhisattva, there are attributes xiang 相, or notions, xiang 想. convincingly argues that the term xiang 相 in Kumārajīva’s translation should be read as xiang 想. of a self, of a person, of beings, or of a lifespan, he is not a Bodhisattva. 若菩薩有我相、人相、衆生相、壽者相，即非菩薩。”For an English translation of this passage from the Sanskrit, see 143. For an overall appraisal of Kumārajīva’s Chinese translation of the sutra, see . (columns 9 and 10). Here the Buddha warns that a bodhisattva who clings to attributes (zhuo xiang 着相) is unable to tame his mind.

Further, the Buddha shows that a bodhisattva who practices giving “should not abide in any dharma 於法應無所住” (column 10).T#235, 8: 749a12. Thus, before the text reaches the first “aisle” between columns 10 and 11, the essential concept of non-abiding (wu zhu 無住) and non-adhereing (wu zhuo 無著) have been articulated.

Continuing his dialogue with Subhūti, the Buddha repeatedly explains that “all attributes without exception are fraudulent. Seeing all attributes as non-attributes—that is, to see the Thus Come One. 凡所有相，皆是虚妄。若見諸相非相，則見如來。” (column 14).T#235, 8: 749a24–25. Practitioners, he says, “should not apprehend any dharma 不應取法” (column 18),T#235, 8: 749b9. because they know that the Thus Come One’s “expounding the dharma is like the Simile of the Raft,The Simile of the Raft implies that the Buddhaʼs teaching is like a raft that is no longer needed once the river has been crossed. The simile traces back to an early discourse in The Collection of the Middle Length Sayings, Majjhima-nikāya , I, 134–135, 173–174. It is also preserved in Chinese translations. T#26 (200), 1: 764b18–c14. T#125(43.5), 2: 759c29–760a26. should abandon the dharma, to say nothing of what does not conform to dharma. 説法，如栰喻者，法尚應捨，何况非法？” (column 19).T#235, 8: 749b11. For a profound study of the sentence see . The Buddha then explains that bodhisattvas “should engender the mind without abiding anywhere. 應無所住而生其心。” (column 30).T#235, 8: 749c22–23. The Buddha here deploys a pattern of argument that is repeated multiple times in the same rhythm, a pattern referred to by commentatorsFor an overall appraisal of Chinese commentaries to this sutra, see . as the “three phrases” (san­ju 三句), i.e, any given x is the same as non-x, and this is what is called x. For example: “The 32 attributes expounded by the Thus Come One are indeed non-marks; therefore they are called the ‘32 attributes.’ 如來説卅二相，即是非相，是名卅二相。” (column 35).T#235, 8: 750a22–23. Or: “The first pāramitā expounded by the Thus Come One is not the supreme pāramitā ; therefore it is called ‘the supreme pāramitā .’ 如來説第一波羅蜜，非第一波羅蜜，是名第一波羅蜜。” (column 38).T#235, 8: 750b12–13. These seemingly paradoxical claims are again an argument for not attaching oneself to any attribute, as attributes have no self and are empty. This negation of attributes culminates in a reference to the jātaka story of Kṣāntivādin in which the Buddha in a former life gave away his entire body piece by piece.

The dialog in this second part ends with the Buddha revealing the title by which his followers should uphold the sutra. Although the sermon’s importance is stated, it is emphasized through the mouth of Subhūti that “the Thus Come One actually did not expound any dharma. 如来无所説法 。” (column 34). The emptiness of all phenomena thus refers even to words of the Buddha—once more emphasising the parable of the raft.

The third and final part of the carved text glorifies the merits of the Mahayana or Greater Vehicle (da sheng 大乘) scripture. Copying, upholding, reciting, and expounding the sutra surpasses any donation, even the offering of one’s own body, as in the jātaka, and is now labelled a practice of the “lesser Dharma (xiao fa 小法).” Then the final carved sentence gives the distinct and clear instruction that the sutra deserves the highest worship by the ritual of circumambulation—just like the bodily relics of the Buddha himself.For worship of the sutra, see The Sutra as Stupa in the present volume, part 1. For a translation of the entire final passage, see p. .

div.head: 經石峪《金剛經》版本之特點
div.p: 經石峪《金剛經》可能刊刻於6世紀70年代，是現存最早的大型石刻本《金剛經》。有關斷代的討論見上文。王樹枏（1851－1936）《新疆訪古録》第11489頁斷定《吐魯番金剛經殘碑》爲北魏。釋永有第151頁認可此説。此碑已佚失，所有斷代無從證實。其原狀録文見魯迅第7册：第1347－1349頁。經石峪刻經前約40年，即東魏天平四年（537），《金剛經》節文266字（；）刊刻於一方造像碑碑陰，其原址傳爲距泰山70公里左右的曲阜市勝果寺。見本卷上册《此經是塔》一文，。
此碑和經石峪《金剛經》一樣，選用鳩摩羅什譯本。其節文對應經石峪《金剛經》嚴重漫漶的末段部分。因此，本卷借助此碑刻復原石經。碑刻本和《大正藏》本之間衹有一字之差，即碑上的兩處“即”字在《大正藏》本爲“則”字。見本卷上册，注和注。

《金剛經》抄本幾乎無助於校補經石峪《金剛經》漫漶部分。梁大同元年（535）《金剛經》殘卷題記雖聲稱使節淳于氏“願造《金剛經》一百份”（），但未見5至6世紀抄寫全本。敦煌研究院藏有一件年款爲“建武四年嵗在丁丑九月朔日”（497年10月12日）的《金剛經》抄本，乃一僞本，見王菡薇。然而，現存紀年爲7世紀的抄本内容僅存經文後段部分，羅慕君第27－28頁所列“有紀年敦煌本《金剛經》編年表”有24號7世紀的殘卷或殘片。其内容缺失，無助於校勘經石峪《金剛經》。

而其他的石刻本《金剛經》則不然。石刻本《金剛經》大多爲鳩摩羅什402年譯本。敦煌藏經洞所見諸多《金剛經》寫本同樣如此。菩提流支（一作菩提留支，活動於508－535年）509年譯本的兩個石刻本則屬於目前所知版本的例外。迄今未見真諦（499－569）、達摩笈多（卒於619年）、玄奘（602－664）或義净（653－713）譯本的石刻本。已知紀年且内容完整的鳩摩羅什譯本石經有三處，均出自7世紀，分布於不同地區：

list.item: 四川省綿陽市，碧水寺，唐貞觀元年（627）。《中國佛教石經·四川省第七卷》。
陝西省淳化縣，金川灣石窟西壁第一層，662－670年之間。《中國佛教石經·陝西省第二卷》。
北京市房山區，石經山第七洞232號石碑，唐垂拱元年（685）。 《房山石經》第2卷：第400－401頁。

div.p: 兩處菩提流支譯本石經刻於7世紀：

list.item: 北京市房山區，石經山雷音洞南壁，隋大業十二年（616）。拓本見《房山石經》第1卷：第88－94頁。刻經所在位置見《雷音洞》第99頁，圖2－93。
河南省洛陽市，龍門石窟擂鼓臺4號窟，即中洞前壁後側，武則天（690－705年在位)時期。録文見《龍門石窟考古報告》第1册：第78－80頁，録文校訂本見同書，第3册：第314－320頁。刻經所在位置見同書，第5册：第113頁，圖1-218。拓片見同書，第5册：第251－252頁，圖1-40和圖1-41。

div.p: 8世紀上半葉，四川省安岳縣卧佛院刊刻三處鳩摩羅什譯本《金剛經》：

list.item: 33號窟。《中國佛教石經·四川省第二卷》。
46號窟。《中國佛教石經·四川省第三卷》。
71號窟。《中國佛教石經·四川省第五卷》。

div.p: 9世紀，還有帶有三十二分標題的鳩摩羅什譯本《金剛經》刻於四川的兩個遺址：

list.item: 四川省綿陽市，北山院，唐乾符七年(880)至中和元年(881)。有關該址的年代，見張總第88－89頁。參見《中國佛教石經·四川省第七卷》。
四川省營山縣，太蓬山（三處刻經有兩處已漫漶）。邵磊和蔣曉春。邵磊第58－61頁。《中國佛教石經·四川省第七卷》。

div.p: 鳩摩羅什譯本除了三十二分本外，石刻本中還有一個十八分本殘篇，見於一塊尺寸约为100厘米×50厘米的不規則四邊形石板，殘存文分三層。此刻經的十八分出自無著（Asaṅga，活動於4世紀）注疏《金剛般若論》。據其形制和書體，此刻經應不早於9世紀：

list.item: 西安碑林，原址不詳。見《中國佛教石經·陝西省第三卷》中陳婷婷之文章。

div.p: 根據上述十部《金剛經》石刻全本，以下展開討論經石峪《金剛經》的三個特點：

經石峪《金剛經》的經題爲《佛説金剛般若波羅蜜經》，而此經梵文版的經題（“ Vajracchedikā prajñāpāramitā ”）并無對應“佛説”的詞。僧祐（445－518）撰《出三藏記集》等所有佛經目録所載《金剛經》的經題均無“佛説”二字。例如，《出三藏記集》T#2145，55：11a7；法經《衆經目録》T#2146，55：117c9；翻經沙門《衆經目録》T#2147，55：158b4；静泰《衆經目録》T#2148，55：192c19；智昇《開元釋教録》T#2154，55：512b9－11。感謝何書群（Michael Radich）指點！此二字多見於6至7世紀鳩摩羅什本寫本，如上文提及的淳于氏寫經（535）和敦煌寫經S.2605（616）。http://idp.bl.uk/database/oo_scroll_h.a4d?uid=52882666517;recnum=2603;index=1（檢索於：2020年12月20日）。金川灣石窟所刻《金剛經》起首也有此二字。見《中國佛教石經·陝西省第二卷》，西壁，圖版1－2a，第71行。經尾簡寫的經題中無此二字，見同書，西壁，圖版1－22a，第199行。 綿陽碧水寺和北京房山所存7世紀石刻本《金剛經》中的經題部分惜已無存。
中觀大師吉藏（549－623）是最早討論“佛説”一詞的學僧。有關吉藏和“佛説”一詞，見船山徹。他在其《金剛般若疏》討論了此經經題的兩種可能性。他認爲經題中應有“佛説”二字，以此區别外道所説經：

quote.p: 經曰：“《金剛般若波羅蜜》。”
釋曰：“經題有二種。一者具足，二者不具足。具足應言‘佛説金剛般若’，不具足但云‘金剛般若’。”
問曰：“餘經何因緣故不題‘佛説’？”
答：“一切諸經佛口自説，皆悉應題爲‘佛説’也。而不題者存略故。”
問曰：“何故此經題爲‘佛説’耶？”
答曰：“《大智論》云：‘有五種人説。一者佛口自説，二者弟子説，三者諸天説，四者仙人説，五化人説。’今此經是佛口自説，非餘人説，是故題‘佛説’也。”
問曰：“餘經亦是佛口自説非餘人説，何故不云‘佛自説’？”

答曰：“已如前説理實應題，以存略故。復次，如《大品》等經，命須菩提説，非佛自説，是故不得題爲‘佛説’，但云《摩訶波若》。《波若》具兼師弟子二説。此經不爾，雖對須菩提而佛自説，非命説也。復次，以理言之，應題‘佛説’。所以者何？諸外道六師等輩亦皆説經，今恐墮邪見，欲簡异六師，故題‘佛説’，使人信受法也。復次，道不孤運，必由人弘。參照《論語·衛靈公》：“子曰：‘人能弘道，非道弘人。’” 《十三經注疏》下册：第2518頁。英譯參見李克曼第77頁。法雖佛師，要由佛説法乃得弘。以是義故題‘能説之人所説之法’，則於義具足也。”

div.p: 因此，經石峪《金剛經》的經題有“佛説”二字理應遵循6和7世紀的通常做法。《金剛經》在中土傳布伊始，“佛説”二字賦予此經權威性，從而“使人信受法也”。
不過，吉藏持論并未普及。上文提及8世紀上半葉刻於卧佛院的三部《金剛經》的經題也無“佛説”二字，《大正藏》及校勘記同樣脱此二字。曇應（活動於12世紀30年代）認爲“此後人謬加”，他在其《金剛般若波羅蜜經釆微餘釋》説明經題無需“佛説”二字：
div.quote: 世傳有加“佛説”二字，謂《佛説金剛般若》等，此後人謬加。須知經通五人説，謂佛、菩薩、弟子、天、仙、化人五人也。若部内兼四人説者，題中不稱“佛説”。若一經始終金口所説者，題標“佛説”字，揀非兼四人所説也。然何以稱經？須知雖四人所説，爲佛印定，即同佛説，故得稱經。《金剛般若波羅蜜經釆微餘釋》R 92：227a4－9。
div.p: 因此，對曇應而言，衹有從頭至尾單獨由佛陀宣説的經文，纔可冠以“佛説”之名。按此説法，衹有極少部分經題有“佛説”二字。像僧祐撰《出三藏記集》衹有六個經題有此二字，《出三藏記集》T#2145，55：16a16：《佛説如是有諸比丘經》；16a18：《佛説道有比丘經》；16a21：《佛説善惡意經一卷》；22a14：《佛説往古性和佛國願行法典經》；22a17：《佛説陀羅尼法門六種動經》；51c19：《佛説無量門微密持經》（《佛説阿難陀目佉尼阿離陀隣尼經》）。有一例作見於兼用經題。《出三藏記集》T#2145，55：27b7：《鹿王經》一卷（或云《佛説昔爲鹿王經》）。而經石峪《金剛經》經題遵循上述另作法。
總之，經石峪《金剛經》的經題并非逐字對應其印度語本，但體現了印度佛教文獻對“經”的定義。鳩摩羅什譯本《金剛經》完成一個半世紀左右後，即《金剛經》在中土的初傳期，經石峪巨岩上“佛説”二大字必定使刻經觀者倍加感同身受。

經石峪《金剛經》選用通用版本的第二個特徵，就我們目前所知，其底本的篇幅小於鳩摩羅什本譯本的後出本子。姜豐榮已於1989年得此結論。姜豐榮第84頁。我們以《大正藏》所收定本比對刻經現存部分，存有十處异文（見本卷録文）。刻經有一處比《大正藏》本多一字，見，注。六處脱字，見，注、注、注、注、注和注。三處异文。見，注、注和注。注爲《大正藏》本排印錯誤，CBETA已訂。 此外，刻經的磨滅部分可容定本20字左右。見，注、注、注和注。
經石峪《金剛經》部分脱字同樣不見於後出版本。“不可以三十二相得見如來”11字（，注）既不見於6至8世紀的石刻本，也不見於僅存的6世紀寫本（，第6行）。這表明，經石峪刊刻的經文并非刻意删减。下文所示，鳩摩羅什譯本的内容與時俱增。
何離巽詳盡比對《金剛經》各種梵文寫本與多個漢文譯本，這或許有助於理解經石峪刻經所依本子的内容何以偏少。何離巽認爲，鳩摩羅什譯本的底本應該是一個較早且偏短的印度語本。何離巽，特别是第218頁和第243頁。經石峪《金剛經》的内容較之7世紀的本子偏少，或許恰好忠實反映了鳩摩羅什本的原貌。
經石峪刻經之後的《金剛經》校訂者在此内容方面做出兩項主要變動：
其一，劃分此經爲三十二分，添入各分標題，并把此舉歸爲昭明太子（501－531）。見上文，。《金剛經》分題石刻本最早出現於上述兩處9世紀刻經址。另外，西安碑林藏有一通無紀年殘碑，刻有十八分《金剛經》。

其二，添加62字《冥司偈》。有關《冥司偈》的綜述，見何秋輝第234－244頁。關於《金剛經》的《冥司偈》和《十王經》之間的緊密聯繫，見太史文第97頁。
此偈明顯出自菩提流支譯本（T#236a，8：756a16－19），添入鳩摩羅什譯本則不晚於唐咸通九年（868）。現存最早、有明確紀年的《金剛經》印本（868年），今藏大英圖書館（編號Or.8210/P.2，正面），上有《冥司偈》（）。如所示，終生熱衷信奉《金剛經》的翟奉達（活動於902－966年)爲一件《金剛經》寫卷謄録了三十二分的分題和《冥司偈》。有關翟奉達及其信奉《金剛經》的相關活動，見何秋輝第53－57頁、第77－78頁、第89－91頁、第49－251頁和第270頁。河南省開封市繁塔内壁於太平興國二年（977）刊刻的《金剛經》何秋輝第242－243頁。和《大正藏》本《金剛經》（T#235，8：751c16－19）也含有此偈。經石峪《金剛經》刊刻於6世紀，其底本必定不含《冥司偈》。
經石峪《金剛經》第三個特版本特色是，第15行有兩處异文頗有意味。此句如下：

quote.p: 如來滅後五百歲，有持戒脩德者……

div.p: 此刻經之後，從7世紀的三個石刻本到《大正藏》本各本，“後”字下有“後”字，“德”字作“福”字：

quote.p: 如來滅後，後五百歲，有持戒脩福者……

div.p:
經石峪《金剛經》脱一“後”字似乎顧及漢語文體的簡潔明瞭。後出《金剛經》本子則遵循漢文佛經常見的四言律，却使“如來滅後”與“後五百嵗”語意含混。這句經文恰好兩次出現於鳩摩羅什譯本《法華經》，可證經石峪《金剛經》行文有獨到之處，而後出本子符合鳩摩羅什譯本《金剛經》原貌。鳩摩羅什的弟子在《法華經》采用此句，凡兩見：
如來滅後，後五百歲……《妙法蓮華經》T#262，9：54b29和62a9。
隋大業十二年（616）刊刻於雷音洞的早期石刻本也可佐證此行文。《妙法蓮華經》T#262，9：54b29，即桐谷征一圖版65倒數第三行。《妙法蓮華經》T#262，9：62a9， 即桐谷征一圖版76第四行。

經石峪刻經第15行的异文“德”，不作“福”，二字在語義學上并無歧義。漢文佛教文獻中的“福”“德”二字和“福德”“功德”二詞所表達的概念基本一致， 對譯同一梵文段落中的 puṇya 或 guṇa 。此梵文段落曰：
api tu khalu punaḥ subhūte bhaviṣyaṃty anāgate 'dhvani bodhisatvā mahāsatvā paści­mā­yāṃ paṃ­ca­śat­yāṃ saddharma­vipra­lope {pe} vartamāne śīlavaṃto guṇavaṃtaḥ prajñā­vaṃto bhaviṣyaṃti (§6).
何離巽和渡邊章悟第116頁。另見維達第76頁作：“ api tu khalu punaḥ subhūte bhaviṣyantyanāgate 'dhvani bodhisattvā mahāsattvāḥ paścime kāle paścime samaye paścimāyāṃ pañcaśatyāṃ saddharmavipralope vartamāne guṇavantaḥ śīlavantaḥ prajñāvantaśca bhaviṣyanti, ye imeṣvevaṃrūpeṣu sūtrāntapadeṣu bhāṣyamāṇeṣu bhūtasaṃjñāmutpāadayiṣyanti ”。 Guṇa 一字見莫尼爾-威廉斯第357頁。

鳩摩羅什譯本原文作“福”，而非“德”。首先，“福德”二字在經石峪本刻經可辨識部分凡九處（第十一、十二和十七行各一次，第二十一、二十二和二十三行各兩次），“福”字凡三見（第二十二、三十七和四十三行），第十五行的“德”字僅乃孤例。其次，不但所有後出本子都作“福”字，而且上文提及的三個7世紀石刻本、《大正藏》本，以及《金剛經》注疏也如此，如智顗著《金剛般若經疏》《金剛般若經疏》T#1698，33：78a1－2：“如來滅後，後五百歲，有持戒修福者。”和吉藏著《金剛般若疏》。《金剛般若疏》T#1699, 33: 105c7－8: “有持戒修福者……”。
如果我們留意下列諸譯本中 “ guṇa ”一詞譯法的具體變化，就會發現，三個後出譯本皆不從鳩摩羅什譯本作“德”字。因此，經石峪刻經具有一定的超前之處。
菩提流支509年譯本作：

quote.p: 有未來世末世，有菩薩摩訶薩，法欲滅時，有持戒修福德智慧者……《金剛般若波羅蜜經》T#236a，8：753a27－b1。
於未來世，後五百歲，正法滅時，有諸菩薩摩訶薩持戒修福及有智慧……《金剛般若波羅蜜經》T#237，8：762b28－c2。《金剛般若波羅蜜經》T#236b，8：757c18－21：“於未來世，後五十歲，正法滅時，有諸菩薩摩訶薩持戒修福及有智慧……”。

div.p: 值得注意的是，其他三種漢譯本《金剛經》譯“ guṇa ” 作“德”：

quote.p: 當有未來世菩薩摩訶薩，後分五百，正法破壞時中，轉時中，戒究竟功德究竟智慧究竟……《金剛能斷般若波羅蜜經》T#238，8：767b14－16。笈多譯本的深入研究，見左冠明。

div.p: 玄奘648年譯本作：

quote.p: 有菩薩摩訶薩於當來世後時後分，後五百歲，正法將滅時分轉時，具足尸羅具德具慧……《大般若波羅蜜多經》T#220(9)，8：980c8－10。
當來之世，有諸菩薩具戒具德具慧……《能斷金剛般若波羅蜜多經》T#239，8：772b9。

div.p: 概而言之，經石峪《金剛經》的本子别具特色，其中一些异文難以解讀。如果我們無從發現更多依據，這些异文孤例則無助於瞭解6世紀《金剛經》譯本的更多變化過程。不過，這個本子的特殊意義在於刊刻在 山水之間，韓文彬第174－175頁。經本在此處熠熠生輝，成爲旋繞儀式的中心。參見本卷上册《此經是塔》一文。

div.head: Special Features of the Text Version
div.p: Probably carved in the 570s, the Diamond Sutra on Mount Tai is the earliest extant large-scale stone version of the text.For the discussion of the date, see above, p. . Wang Shunan 王樹枏 (1851–1936), 11489 dates the so-called Fragmentary Stele of the Diamond Sutra from Turfan to the Northern Wei (385–535). 151, agrees with the dating. Since the stele is lost, the date cannot be verified. For a transcription of the stele with the text layout, see 7: 1347–1349. About four decades earlier, in 537, a section of the sutra with 266 characters was carved on the back of a votive stele from Sheng­guo Monastery, only about 70 km away from Mount Tai (figs. and ).See the article The Sutra as Stupa in the present volume, part 1, p. .

figure.head: Manuscript of the Diamond Sutra from Shanshan, colophon, dated 535; Calligraphy Museum of Taitō District, Tōkyō; after , 142–143
《金剛經》寫本題記，紀年535年，鄯善出土，東京臺東區立書道博物館藏。采自《中村不折旧藏》第142－143頁

div.p:
Like the text in Sutra Stone Valley, the carving on the stele uses Kumā­rajīva’s translation. It corresponds to the final, heavily damaged section in the Valley, and we have relied on the text on the stele for its reconstruction. There is only one variant: the character ze 則 (then) in the
Taishō canon is on the stele twice replaced by the character ji 即 (namely).See p. , notes and .

When trying to reconstruct other damaged passages in Sutra Stone Valley, written manuscripts are of limited use. Although we learn from the colophon of 535 on the manuscript by envoy Chunyu (fig. ) that the sutra was copied on paper in large numbers, no complete dated manuscript from the fifth and sixth centuries has survived.A forged manuscript dated October 12, 497 is preserved in the Dunhuang Academy; see also . Traceable manuscripts from the seventh century only have the sutra’s final sections, 27–28 lists 24 fragmentary manuscripts of the seventh century from Dunhuang. which, unfortunately, do not overlap enough with the text on Mount Tai to make a critical edition of the carved text possible.

Other versions of the Diamond Sutra carved on stone are useful, however. Our survey of these carvings has shown that the majority were based on Kumā­rajīva’s translation of 402. The same can be said of the numerous versions found in the library in Dunhuang. Two engraved examples of Bodhiruci’s 菩提流支/菩提留支 (fl. 508–535) translation of 509 that are known to us so far are exceptions. None of the translations by Paramārtha 真諦 (499–569), Dharmagupta 笈多 (died 619), Xuanzang 玄奘 (602–664), or Yijing 義净 (635–713) have been found on stone. Yet three complete and clearly dated stone versions of Kumā­rajīva’s translation are known from the seventh century. They were all carved in different regions:

list.item: Bishui Monastery 碧水寺, Mianyang City 綿陽市, Sichuan, 627 (first year of the zhenguan era 貞觀元年). .
Jinchuanwan Cave 金川灣石窟, register 1, west wall, Chunhua County 淳化縣, Shaanxi, can be dated sometime between 662 and 670. .
Stone Sutra Mountain (Shijing Shan 石經山), cave 7, stele no. 232, Fang Shan 房山, Beijing, dated 685 (first year of the chui­gong era 垂拱元年). 2: 400–401.

div.p: The two engraved versions of the sutra in Bo­dhi­ru­ci’s translation also date from this time:

list.item: Stone Sutra Mountain (Shijing Shan 石經山), Thunder Sound Cave (Leiyin Dong 雷音洞), south wall, Fang Shan 房山, Beijing, dated 616 (twelfth year of the daye 大業十二年).For rubbings of the text, see 1: 88–94. For the position of the text in the cave, see 99, fig. 2–93.

Leigu Tai 擂鼓臺, cave no. 4, also known as Middle Cave 中洞; inner side of the front wall, Longmen 龍門; era of Wu Zetian 武則天 (Wu Zhao 武曌, 624–705, r. as Emperor 690–705).A transcription of the text in 1: 78–80; transcription with text critical apparatus ibid., 3: 314–320; location of the text in the cave ibid., 5: 113, fig. 1-218; rubbings of the text ibid., 5: 251–252, figs. 1-40 and 1-41.

div.p: During the first half of the eighth century, three versions of the Diamond Sutra in Kumā­rajīva’s translation were carved at the Grove of the Reclining Buddha (Wofo Yuan 卧佛院) in Anyue County 安岳縣, Sichuan:

list.item: Cave 33. .
Cave 46. .
Cave 71. .

div.p: In the ninth century, more versions of Kumā­rajīva’s translation with a division into 32 sections were carved at two other sites in Sichuan:

list.item: Beishan Yuan 北山院, Mianyang City 綿陽市, 880–881.For a discussion on the dating of the site, see 88–89. See also .
Mount Taipeng 太蓬山, Yingshan County 營山縣; two of three versions are severely damaged. ; 58–61; .

div.p: A fragmentary version of Kumā­rajīva’s translation on an irregularly shaped vertical rectangular stone slab measuring ca. 100 × 50 cm, with three registers of text remaining shows the division of 18 sections from the commentary on the sutra by Asaṅga (Wuzhuo 無著, fl. fourth century BCE). According to its format and calligraphic style, this stone is not earlier than the ninth century:

list.item: Xi’an Stele Forest (Xi’an Beilin 西安碑林), original location not known.See the article by Chen Tingting in .

div.p: With these ten complete versions of the text in view, we can now turn to three special features of the Diamond Sutra on Mount Tai.

div.head: The two Characters “Spoken by the Buddha (Fo shuo 佛説)” in the Sutra Title
div.p: The sutra title carved in the Sutra Stone Valley reads literally: Ada­mant Perfection of Wisdom Sutra Spoken by the Buddha (Fo shuo jin­gang ban­re bo­luo­mi jing 佛説金剛般若波羅蜜經). However, there is no equivalent for the two characters “spoken by the Buddha” in the Sanskrit title Vajra­cchedikā prajñāpāramitā . Accordingly, all catalogs of the Buddhist canon, including the earliest extant one, Sengyou’s 僧祐 (445–518) Collection of the Records of Issuing the three Storehouses, Chu san­zang ji ji 出三藏記集, list the sutra title without these two characters.For instances, T#2145, 55: 11a7; T#2146, 55: 117c9; T#2147, 55: 158b4; T#2148, 55: 192c19; T#2154, 55: 512b9-11. I am grateful to Michael Radich for pointing this out. The characters were, however, common in the title attached to Kumā­rajīva’s translation during the sixth and the seventh centuries. The already mentioned manuscript by Chunyu, dated to 535, contains them, as does the Dunhuang manuscript S.2605, dated 616.See the International Dunhuang Project: http://idp.bl.uk/database/oo_scroll_h.a4d?uid=52882666517;recnum=2603;index=1 (accessed December 20, 2020). The two characters also appear at the beginning of the text in the Jinchuanwan Cave.Column 71 in register 1–2a in . The two characters are not repeated in the abbreviated title at the end of the sutra. Column 199 in register 122a in . In the other two carved seventh century versions, at Bishui Monastery and at Fang Shan, the part with the title is not preserved.

The influential master of the Middle Way (zhongguan 中觀, Madhyamaka), Jizang 吉藏 (549–623), was the earliest commentator to commemt on the term Fo shuo.For Jizang and the term, see . He discussed in his Commentary on the Diamond Sutra (Jingang banre shu 金剛般若疏) the two possibilities. He defended the use of the two characters “spoken by the Buddha” in the title, because they set such a sutra apart from those spoken by heretic followers. He argues:

quote.p: The sutra says: “ Vajra­cchedikā prajñāpāramitā (Perfection of Wisdom which Cuts Like Ada­mant, or: Perfection of Wisdom [ca­pa­ble of] Cutting Adamant).”See above, note . 經曰：“《金剛般若波羅蜜》。”

The exegesis says: “There are two kinds of sutra title. One is complete, while the other is incomplete. The complete one should read Ada­mant Wisdom Spoken by the Buddha. The incomplete one says only Ada­mant Wisdom.” 釋曰：“經題有二種。一者具足，二者不具足。具足應言‘佛説金剛般若’，不具足但云‘金剛般若’。”
Question: “For what reasons do other sutras not have ‘Spoken by the Buddha’ in their titles?” 問曰：“餘經何因緣故不題‘佛説’？”

Answer: “All sutras are spoken by the mouth of the Buddha himself and thus should have ‘Spoken by the Buddha’ in their titles. Not having this in the title is only an abbreviation.”答：“一切諸經佛口自説，皆悉應題爲‘佛説’也。而不題者存略故。”
Question: “Why does this sutra have ‘Spoken by the Buddha’ in its title?” 問曰：“何故此經題爲‘佛説’耶？”

Answer: “The Treatise on the Great [Perfection of] Wisdom says: [The scriptures] are spoken by five kinds of persons: first those spoken by the mouth of the Buddha himself, second those spoken by his disciples, third those spoken by devas, fourth those spoken by sages ( ṛṣi ), and fifth those spoken by [devas, Bodhisattvas, or Buddhas incarnated as] transformed persons.’ Now this sutra is spoken by the mouth of the Buddha himself and not by other persons. Therefore, its title has ‘Spoken by the Buddha.’” 答曰：“《大智論》云：‘有五種人説。一者佛口自説，二者弟子説，三者諸天説，四者仙人説，五化人説。’今此經是佛口自説，非餘人説，是故題‘佛説’也。”
Question: “Other sutras are also spoken by the mouth of the Buddha himself and not by other persons. Why are they not called ‘Spoken by the Buddha himself’?” 問曰：“餘經亦是佛口自説非餘人説，何故不云‘佛自説’？”

Answer: “As already mentioned, they should indeed have in their titles [‘Spoken by the Buddha’], but, because of an abbreviation [they do not]. Furthermore, there are sutras such as the Large Chapter [from the Perfection of Wisdom Sutras] that the Buddha ordered Subhūti to speak and did not speak himself. Therefore, it cannot have ‘Spoken by the Buddha’ in its title, but reads only Mahāprajñā . The Prajñā is spoken by both, master and disciple. Yet this [Diamond] sutra is not like this. It was spoken toward Subhūti by the Buddha himself, and it was not ordered to be spoken. Therefore it is reasonable that it should have ‘Spoken by the Buddha’ in its title. Why is this so? The heretic Six Masters and their ilk also speak sutras. Now, being afraid to lapse into false views, we need to simplify the [titles of the] Six Masters and set them apart. Thus, we put ‘Spoken by the Buddha,’ in the titles in order to have people believe in and receive the Dharma. Furthermore, the Way does not run by itself, it has to be propagated by humans.Cf. the Analects (Lunyu, Duke Ling of Wei 論語·衛靈公): “The Master said, ‘Man can enlarge the Way. It is not the Way that enlarges man.’ 子曰：‘人能弘道，非道弘人。’” 2: 2518. Translation in 77. 英譯見李克曼第77頁。
Although the Dharma is the teacher of the Buddha, it can only become propagated by the Buddha when he speaks the Dharma. On account of this reasoning, [the sutra] has in its title ‘Dharma Spoken by the person who is able to speak it,’ and thus its meaning is complete. 答曰：“已如前説理實應題，以存略故。復次，如《大品》等經，命須菩提説，非佛自説，是故不得題爲‘佛説’，但云《摩訶波若》。《波若》具兼師弟子二説。此經不爾，雖對須菩提而佛自説，非命説也。復次，以理言之，應題‘佛説’。所以者何？諸外道六師等輩亦皆説經，今恐墮邪見，欲簡异六師，故題‘佛説’使人信受法也。復次，道不孤運，必由人弘。法雖佛師，要由佛説法乃得弘。以是義故題‘能説之人所説之法’，則於義具足也。”

div.p: We may therefore conclude that the title of the text in Sutra Stone Valley conforms to the common practice of the sixth and seventh centuries. In this initial period in the dissemination of the text in China the words “spoken by the Buddha” lent authority to the sutra “in order to have people believe in and receive the Dharma 使人信受法也.”

Nevertheless, Jizang’s argument was not widely accepted. In the three versions from the first half of the eighth century in the Grove of the Reclining Buddha listed above, the two characters “spoken by the Buddha” no longer appear. Nor are they seen in the Taishō canon or its witnesses.T#235, 8: 748c17. Latter commentators do not regard the addition of the two characters to be necessary. According to Tan­ying 曇應 (fl. 1130s), for instance, “they were added erroneously by later people 此後人謬加.” He explains in his Supplementary Exegesis to Selected Subtleties of the Diamond Sutra (Jingang jing cai wei yu shi 金剛般若波羅蜜經釆微餘釋) why they are not necessary in the title of the sutra:
div.quote: Among the manuscripts handed down in the world some are with the two additional characters “spoken by the Buddha,” namely Adamant Wisdom Spoken by the Buddha and so on. The characters were added erroneously by later people. You must know that sutras are spoken by five [kinds of] persons, namely Buddhas as well as Bodhisattvas, disciples, devas, sages, and transformed beings. If a text that was spoken jointly by the latter four [kinds of] persons, it cannot have “spoken by the Buddha” in its title. But if a sutra was spoken from beginning to end by the golden mouth [of the Buddha], then that is marked in the title with the characters “spoken by the Buddha,” in order to single out that it was not spoken jointly by the other four [kinds of] persons. Yet, why are those still called sutras? You must know that, though they are spoken by the other four [kinds of] persons, they are certified by the Buddha and are thus equal to those spoken by the Buddha. Therefore, they get to be called sutras. 世傳有加“佛説”二字，謂《佛説金剛般若》等，此後人謬加。須知經通五人説，謂佛、菩薩、弟子、天、仙、化人五人也。若部内兼四人説者，題中不稱“佛説”。若一經始終金口所説者，題標“佛説”字，揀非兼四人所説也。然何以稱經？須知雖四人所説，爲佛印定，即同佛説，故得稱經。 R 92: 227a4–9.
div.p: For Tanying, therefore, only those sutras that are spoken exclusively by the Buddha from the beginning to the end can be entitled with the two characters “spoken by the Buddha.” According to this reasoning, only a few sutras can have the two characters in their titles. For instance in Sengyou’s substantial Compilation of Notes on the Issuing of the Tripitaka, only six sutra titles have the two characters,T#2145, 55: 16a16: 佛説如是有諸比丘經; T#2145, 55: 16a18: 佛説道有比丘經; T#2145, 55: 16a21: 佛説善惡意經一卷; T#2145, 55: 22a14: 佛説往古性和佛國願行法典經; T#2145, 55: 22a17: 佛説陀羅尼法門六種動經; T#2145, 55: 51c19: 佛説無量門微密持經(佛説阿難陀目佉尼阿離陀隣尼經). one title listing an alternative title with the two characters.T#2145, 55: 27b7: 鹿王經一卷(或云佛説昔爲鹿王經). Yet the sutra title in Sutra Stone Valley follows the other tradition mentioned above.

To sum up, the title in Sutra Stone Valley does not reflect an Indic title exactly, but conveys the weight of what is considered to be a sutra in Indic Buddhist textual traditions. Carved in the initial period of the dissemination of the Diamond Sutra in China, i.e. about one and half centuries after the completion of the translation, the large characters with the additional words “spoken by the Buddha” on the huge rock surface must have made the sutra carving seem more potent to its audience.

div.head: Shorter Text
div.p:
A second special feature of the text in Sutra Stone Valley is that its master copy was, as far as we can judge, shorter than all other later versions of Kumā­rajīva’s translation.Jiang Fengrong already in 1989 got the conclusion. 84. There are ten differences between the surviving carved text and received version in the Taishō canon (see the transcription pp. ). In only one case, the carved text has an additional character,See p. , note . but in six cases the carved text has fewer characters,See p. , notes , , , , , and . and in three cases the carved text is entirely different.See p. , notes , , and . Note is a typo of the Taishō canon, which has already been corrected in CBETA.
In addition, the damaged part of the carved text lacks space for about 20 characters of the received version.See p. , notes , , , and .

Some characters missing in Sutra Stone Valley are also lacking in later versions. For instance, the eleven characters “不可以三十二相得見如來” (p. , note ) are not found in the stone versions from the seventh and eighth centuries mentioned above, nor in column 6 of the only extant manuscript of the sixth century (fig. ). This shows that the text in Sutra Stone Valley was not arbitrarily shortened. On the contrary, as we will see, Kumā­rajīva’s translation was expanded through time.

Paul Harrison’s detailed comparison of various Sanskrit manuscripts and the Chinese translations of the Diamond Sutra perhaps holds a clue for understanding why the text in Sutra Stone Valley is shorter. Harrison argues that Kumā­rajīva’s translation may represent an earlier and shorter Sanskrit version of the sutra. , especially pages 218 and 243. The text in Sutra Stone Valley, which is shorter than the seventh century versions, may reflect Kumā­rajīva’s original translation to some extent.

Later editors introduced two main changes to the text of the Diamond Sutra. They inserted the titles of the 32 sections attributed to crown-prince Zhao­ming 昭明太子 (501–531).See above, . These section headings appeared on stone first in the two texts carved in the ninth century mentioned above. (A different division in 18 sections is seen in the undated fragment in the Stele Forest in Xi’an.)

figure.head: 《金剛經》寫本殘片，紙本，26.5厘米×20厘米。敦煌出土，6世紀。中國國家圖書館藏，BD15894。采自《國家圖書館藏敦煌遺書》第145册：第25頁

div.p:
The second later addition to the text are the 62 characters of the so-called Gāthā from the Underworld Bureau (Ming­si ji 冥司偈).For a thorough discussion on the
Gāthā
234–244. On its existence in the Diamond Sutra that was bound together with the Scripture on the Ten Kings (Shi­wang jing 十王經), see 97. These were evidently taken from Bodhiruci’s translation (T#236a, 8: 756a16–19) and, by 868,The earliest extant printed book with the precise date 868, the Diamond Sutra preserved in the British Library (numbered Or.8210/P.2 Recto) contains the Gāthā (fig. ). inserted into Kumā­rajīva’s translation. As shown in fig. , in 908, the zealous and lifelong promoter of the cult of the Diamond Sutra Zhai Fengda 翟奉達 (fl. 902–966) added a manuscript of the sutra, in addition to the 32 section headings, with the Gāthā .For Zhai Fengda and his activities concerning the cult of the Diamond Sutra as well as further references, see 53–57, 77–78, 89–91, 249–251, and 270. In 977, the Gāthā was included in the stone version of the Diamond Sutra carved on the inner wall of the Po Pagoda 繁塔 at Kaifeng 開封, Henan, 242–243. and it is now also seen in the Taishō canon (T#235, 8: 751c16–19). This Gāthā from the Underworld Bureau was certainly not part of the master copy that the monks in Sutra Stone Valley used in the sixth century.

figure.head: 《金剛經》寫本，紙本，局部，含翟奉達（活動於902－966年）唐天復八年（908）添寫文字，敦煌出土。法國國家圖書館東方手稿部藏P.2094號。采自gallica.bnf.fr

The Diamond Sutra with supplements by Zhai Fengda (fl. 902–966) in 908, scroll, ink on paper, detail, Dunhuang, Gansu; Bibliothèque nationale de France, Paris (Pelliot chinois 2094); source: gallica.bnf.fr

div.head: Unique Readings
div.p: The third special feature is two significant variants in a phrase in column 15 in Sutra Stone Valley. The carved version reads:
