div.quote: Five hundred years after the Buddha’s extinction, if there is someone who upholds the precepts and cultivates merit (de) . . . 如來滅後五百歲，有持戒脩德者……
div.p: All later editions, from the three stone versions in the seventh century, to the version in the Taishō canon add a second character hou 後 and write fu 福 instead of de 德: After the Buddha’s extinction, in the final five hundred years, if there is someone who upholds the precepts and cultivates merit (fu) . . . 如來滅後，後五百歲，有持戒脩福者……

It seems that the omission of hou in the Valley is to conform to a terse, concise, indigenous Chinese style. The latter witnesses, however, comply with the tetrasyllabic meter 四言律 of Chinese sutra texts, though the time span between the Buddha’s death and the final 500 years becomes vague. The Lotus Sutra, also translated by Kumā­rajīva, proves that the reading in the Valley is an exception and the later witnesses bear the original reading of Kumā­rajīva’s translation. His team uses the same phrase twice in the Lotus Sutra:
div.quote:
After the Buddha’s extinction, in the final five hundred years . . . 如來滅後，後五百歲
…… T#262, 9: 54b29; T#262, 9: 62a9.
div.p: The early stone version of the Lotus Sutra carved in Thunder Sound Cave by 616 confirms this reading.T#262, 9: 54b29 = the last third column in pl. 65. T#262, 9: 62a9 = the fourth column in pl. 76.

Semantically speaking, the second variant in the phrase in column 15, de 德 instead of fu 福, is not in fact a variant. In Buddhist Chinese, both terms, as well as the terms fude 福德 and gongde 功德, convey the general idea of “merit,”
puṇya , or guṇa (good quality, virtues, merit, excellence) 357. as in the Sanskrit phrase:
api tu khalu punaḥ subhūte bhaviṣyaṃty anāgate ’dhvani bodhisatvā mahāsatvā paści­mā­yāṃ paṃcaśatyāṃ saddharma­vipra­lope {pe} vartamāne śīlavaṃto guṇavaṃtaḥ prajñāvaṃto bhaviṣyaṃti (§6).
116. 76 reads: “ api tu khalu punaḥ subhūte bhaviṣyantyanāgate ’dhvani bodhisattvā mahāsattvāḥ paścime kāle paścime samaye paścimāyāṃ pañcaśatyāṃ saddharmavipralope vartamāne guṇavantaḥ śīlavantaḥ prajñāvantaśca bhaviṣyanti, ye imeṣvevaṃrūpeṣu sūtrāntapadeṣu bhāṣyamāṇeṣu bhūtasaṃjñāmutpāadayiṣyanti .”

Again, fu 福 seems to have been the reading of Kumā­rajīva’s original translation, because, firstly, in the discernible parts of the text in the Valley we see nine uses of fude 福德 (cols 11, 12, 17, 21 twice, 22 twice, and 32 twice) and three times fu 福 (cols 22, 37, and 43), whereas de 德 in column 15 is an exception. Secondly, all later editions read fu 福, beginning with the three stone versions in the seventh century, and including the version in the Taishō canon. So do the commentaries on the sutra, such as Zhiyi’s 智顗 (538–597) Commentary on the Diamond Sutra (Jingang banre jing shu 金剛般若經疏)T#1698, 33: 78a1–2: “如來滅後後五百歲有持戒修福者。” and Jizang’s Commentary on the Diamond [Sutra] (Jingang banre shu 金剛般若疏).T#1699, 33: 105c7–8: “有持戒修福者…….”

If we see the evolution of how the term guṇa is rendered in other translations as detailed below, it appears coincidental that the reading on Mount Tai is ahead of its time, since the later three translations did not follow Kumā­rajīva’s translation and read de 德:
Bodhiruci, 509, reads:
div.quote: In the final time, at a future time, there will be bodhisattva mahāsattvas, when Dharma is about to be extinguished, there will be someone who upholds precepts and cultivates merit (fude) and wisdom . . . 有未來世末世，有菩薩摩訶薩，法欲滅時，有持戒修福德智慧者……”T#236a, 8: 753a27–b1.
div.p: Paramārtha 真諦 (499–569), 562, reads:
div.quote: At a future time, in the final five hundred years, when True Dharma is extinguished, there will be bodhisattva mahāsattvas who uphold precepts, cultivate merit (fu) and possess wisdom . . . 於未來世，後五百歲，正法滅時，有諸菩薩摩訶薩持戒修福及有智慧……”T#237, 8: 762b28–c2. Paramārtha, T#236b, 8: 757c18–21: “At a future time, in the final fifty years, when True Dharma is extinguished, there will be bodhisattva mahāsattvas who uphold precepts, cultivate merit (fu) and possess wisdom . . ..於未來世，後五十歲，正法滅時，有諸菩薩摩訶薩持戒修福及有智慧…….”

figure.head: 《金剛經》，卷軸，紙本墨印，局部，敦煌出土，唐咸通九年木刻本。大英圖書館藏品（編號Or.8210/P.2）。采自www.bl.uk
The Diamond Sutra, scroll, woodblock print, ink on paper, detail, Dunhuang, Gansu, dated 868. British Library, London (Or.8210/P.2 Recto). Source: www.bl.uk

div.p: Interestingly, in the other three translations, here also arranged in chronological order, guṇa is translated as de 德:
Dharmagupta 笈多 (?–619), 605, reads:
div.quote:
There will be, in a future time, in the final portion of five hundred [years], in the time when the True Dharma is being destroyed, while this process is unfolding,I am grateful to Michael Radich for the translation of this phrase. He explains that the obscure expression 轉時中 (in the time of revolving) is probably an attempt to render the Sanskrit
vartamāṇe more literally, which 144 translates as "coming to pass." Vr̥t = zhuan 轉 means "turn, revolve," but has extended senses meaning "take place, happen," etc. there will be bodhisattva mahāsattvas whose precepts are complete, merit (gongde) is complete, and wisdom is complete . . . 當有未來世菩薩摩訶薩，後分五百，正法破壞時中，轉時中，戒究竟功德究竟智慧究竟……”T#238, 8: 767b14–16. For a profound study of Dharmagupta’s translation, see .
div.p: Xuanzang, 648, reads:
div.quote: There will be bodhisattva mahāsattvas at a future time, in the final time, the final portion, in the final five hundred years, when the time of the imminent extinction of the True Dharma is underway,Xuanzang has the similar obscure expression 轉時 (in the time of revolving). See above. who are fully endowed with precepts, merit (de), and wisdom . . . 有菩薩摩訶薩於當來世後時後分，後五百歲，正法將滅時分轉時，具足尸羅具德具慧…… T#220(9), 8: 980c8–10.
div.p: Yìjìng 義净 (635–713), 703, reads:
div.quote: In a future time, there will be bodhisattvas who are been endowed with precepts, merit (de), and wisdom . . . 當來之世，有諸菩薩具戒具德具慧……”T#239, 8: 772b9.
div.p: To conclude, the text in the Sutra Stone Valley is unique. It is, however, difficult to interpret some of the variant readings. Until we can find more evidence, they remain unexplained and cannot provide more insight into the textual history of the Diamond Sutra in the sixth century. Rather, the true significance of this version stems from the fact that the carved sutra is embedded in a landscape, 174–175. from where the text radiates its luminous power and functions as a hub for the ritual of circumambulation.See the article The Sutra as Stupa in the present volume, part 1.
